As has already been expressed by the variety of briefs and witnesses, this is a complex and tragic public health emergency with multiple viewpoints and considerations. It is also a topic of great urgency to Canadians, with over 47,000 lives lost to opioid-related toxicity since 20161. [ORG] is eager to support a submission from the national perspective of addiction medicine providers. Evidence-based policy requires expert and specialist points of view along with the added insight of the etiology and epidemiology of those at risk of, or living with, substance use disorders (SUDs). SUDs are best understood as chronic medical conditions, with multiple biopsychosocial factors underlying their development, often in adolescence or early adulthood, not through a moral failing or a criminal justice lens. A previous estimate of lifetime prevalence of a SUD2 in Canada is shockingly high, at 21%, or over 1 in 5 Canadians. Additionally, according to CIHI3, SUDs were the 5th most common cause of hospitalization in 2021-2022, reflecting a substantial impact on an already taxed medical system. card_text: Over the Influence Brief to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health The Opioid Epidemic and Toxic Drug Crisis As the opioid epidemic and toxic drug crisis take deeper roots in Canada, youth have become a largely affected population with little to no support. According to the Government of Canada (2024), nearly 20% of total apparent opioid toxicity deaths were youth aged 0-to-29 years old. The impact on young people in Canada is profound and multifaceted, as the crisis impacts their health, wellbeing, and prospects. According to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), youth who engage in substance misuse are more likely to experience physical and sexual dating violence and are more susceptible to mental health and suicide risk (Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, 2020). This epidemic has far-reaching effects on the youth demographic and is disrupting the lives and futures of our future leaders. As opioid-related poisonings and deaths escalate, there has been an uptake in interest around interventions. However, even though Canadians are being introduced to opioids and addiction at an earlier age, most interventions are based around the adult (25+) experience. According to Corace et al. (2018), a majority of the related research is in reference to adults, even though youth were 3-4 times more likely than adults 25 and older to report harm related to substance use in their physical health, social and work/study, legal, and housing problems. These interventions cannot adopt a one-size-fits-all approach—youth have unique needs that require specialized prevention,

card_text: Policy Proposal: Addressing Drug Addiction through Comprehensive Legal and Supportive Measures Author: T. Lewis Federal Policy Analyst (Submitting as a Concerned Private Citizen with Indigenous family members) Date: August 9, 2024 Policy Proposal: Addressing Drug Addiction through Comprehensive Legal and Supportive Measures Author: T. Lewis Policy Analyst Executive Summary The increasing prevalence of illicit drug use and its associated societal impacts represent a pressing humanitarian crisis in Ontario. Public intoxication with illicit drugs has severe implications for individual health, community safety, and societal well-being. This policy proposal advocates for making public intoxication with illicit drugs illegal and introduces a compassionate, comprehensive diversion program that mandates treatment over punishment. The goal is to transform the current punitive approach into a rehabilitative one, emphasizing the restoration of human potential through state-of-the-art treatment facilities and robust support systems. Problem Statement Ontario's municipalities are overwhelmed by the twin crises of homelessness and drug addiction, exacerbated by inadequate and unpredictable funding from provincial and federal governments. The loss of human potential due to illicit drug use, evidenced by over 2,500 annual overdose deaths in Ontario alone, demands an urgent and multifaceted response. Current ad-hoc funding and fragmented efforts are insufficient to address the deepening crisis, necessitating a structured and compassionate approach to both law enforcement and rehabilitation. Objectives 1. Make Public Intoxication with Illicit Drugs Illegal - Introduce legislation that prohibits public intoxication with illicit drugs to enhance public safety and community well-being. 2. Implement a Compassionate Diversion Program - Develop a court diversion program

card_text: Written Submission for the Standing Committee on Health Opioid Epidemic and Toxic Drug Crisis in Canada On behalf of The [ORG] May 24, 2024 Context: Towns and cities across Canada are experiencing high rates of drug overdose and death. Experts, policy makers and community service agencies have expressed their views on what needs to be done and have been working on doing it. There are polarizing views on how to solve this devastating societal issue in Canada. Any and all of the solutions are complex. Some propose better support for youth experiencing difficulties to help prevent their need to explore substances as a coping strategy. Others feel the solution is in making sure that people have a safe supply of drugs so that we can keep them alive long enough to convince them to try treatment supports, while others propose that we focus primarily on the recovery for those struggling with substance use. Likely, we need to be doing a combination of all of these things and more. Many of the solutions that we frequently hear about are based on the assumption that people using substances are fully functional when they are not using substances. This is important because programs and policy to address substance use in Canada are often built on this premise. We know that there are people with disabilities, and specifically those with Fetal Alcohol Spectrum Disorder (FASD), who are often unrecognized, undiagnosed and unsupported. When this happens, we know that people with FASD are much more

card_text: [ORG] is an organization that provides health care to people who are homeless or vulnerably housed1. We offer a wide variety of shelter based and supportive housing-based social-determinants of health services for individuals, the vast majority of whom use drugs. Since 2001, we have been leading responses to changes in Ottawa’s unregulated, non-prescribed drugs market including the recent acceleration of changes to what we now call the toxic drug supply. It is important to understand that situation on the ground across Canada is evolving rapidly and our collective response is lagging. While the strategies of a decade ago continue to mitigate some of the harms, that mitigation has become less effective due to changes in the content and potency of this drug supply, the social safety net, brain and drug clearance physiology from aging & repeated use and in the behaviour of some of those most impacted. This is compounded by lack of meaningful investment in services to support chronic pain, substance use and mental health including complex trauma treatment. The Opioid Crisis, Phase 1: The Overprescription Crisis Only 20 years ago, there were approximately 100 people in Ottawa on methadone and one of our first roles was to advocate for improved access to methadone. This was the first wave of the opioid crisis, where opioid deaths were primarily driven by prescribed and diverted OxyContin. card_text: Overview: The Standing Committee on Health adopted a motion to undertake a study on the Opioid Epidemic and Toxic Drug Crisis in Canada and the response by federal, provincial, and territorial governments. A challenge Canadians face that must be addressed are the lack of access to non-opioid (non-pharmaceutical) related options to treat and manage pain. The Canadian Pain Task Force’s (CPTF) final report, An Action Plan for Pain in Canada, found that the lack of access to non-opioid options to treat pain is one of the factors contributing to the opioid crisis.i The [ORG] recommends the government take the following steps to ensure that non-opioid related options are available to Canadians seeking care: • Recommendation: For the government to initiate a comparative review of the Non-Insured Health Benefits (NIHB) program, to identify areas to improve Indigenous peoples’ access to care, including reinstating coverage for chiropractic care. • Recommendation: Federal leadership on addressing the burden of MSK conditions by funding pilot projects that demonstrate innovative approaches to treat or manage pain in Indigenous communities. This brief is submitted with the hope of providing clarity about these clinical resources and related public health policy to ensure recommendations made by your committee are based on fact. More information on the clinical protocols and their development is provided below. However, it is important to note that the clinical protocols being referenced do not target youth populations specifically. Additionally, the emphasis on youth access is completely untrue – no youth under the age of 19 has been prescribed fentanyl as an alternative to toxic unregulated drugs since the protocols were published. Prescribed safer supply is considered one public health intervention and harm reduction approach to help reduce or eliminate reliance on the unregulated drug supply. card_text: MORE THAN 130 EXPERTS IN SUBSTANCE USE CALL FOR SCALE UP OF SAFER SUPPLY PROGRAMS December 14, 2023 The Honourable Mark Holland Minister of Health House of Commons Ottawa, ON [CONTACT] The Honourable Ya’ara Saks Minister of Addiction and Mental Health House of Commons Ottawa, ON [CONTACT] Dear Minister Holland and Minister Saks, As researchers and clinicians across Canada, we are writing out of concern regarding the increased politicization surrounding the response to the drug toxicity overdose crisis that is taking the lives of 21 Canadians every day. We are particularly concerned about the spread of misinformation and the denial of the evidence-base on harm reduction interventions, such as prescribed safer supply programs. The ongoing drug toxicity overdose crisis needs to be recognized as a public health emergency driven by an unregulated and volatile drug supply composed of fentanyl, fentanyl analogues and other contaminants of uncertain dose and composition. Prescribed safer supply programs aim to reduce overdose by providing regulated, pharmaceutical drugs of known potency and consistency to people who use unregulated drugs. These programs have been piloted on a small-scale since 2020 to provide a new option for people at high risk of overdose and for whom traditional treatment options like opioid agonist treatments (OAT) have not worked or provided sufficient benefit. Research demonstrates positive impacts of safer supply programs Currently, there are 24 prescribed safer supply programs across Canada receiving federal funding for operations. Context The strength and resilience of Indigenous Peoples in Canada is rooted within a rich diversity of cultures, traditions, and values that have long been nurtured by Indigenous health systems and healing practices. Despite this, Indigenous people across generations have been and continue to be deeply affected by the ongoing legacy of settler colonialism, institutionalized racism, and systemic discrimination in Canada. The final reports from the Truth and Reconciliation Commission (TRC) and the Missing and Murdered Indigenous Women and Girls (MMIWG) Inquiry demonstrated how manifestations of cultural oppression, structural violence, and abuse have resulted in long-lasting health impacts, intergenerational trauma, and economic and social marginalization for Indigenous peoples in this country. These systems of oppression have affected a range of social determinants of health, including income, education, employment, social status, working and living conditions, health practices, and child development. When these social determinants of health are affected, this is linked to higher rates of problematic substance use; physical abuse and violence; living in poverty; and housing insecurity and homelessness.i As stated in

[ORG] has a core membership of between 40 to 60 peers who are current or former illicit alcohol users living in and around the Downtown Eastside. [ORG] defines illicit drinking as the consumption of non-beverage alcohols (including mouthwash, hand sanitizer, and rubbing alcohol) and the use of beverage alcohol in ways that are criminalized, most often due to coerced use of public space due to homelessness, discrimination by service providers, or residence in a Single Room Occupancy (SRO) hotel. [ORG] members are usually long-term, heavy drinkers who meet established criteria for Alcohol Use Disorder (AUD) and who are likely to experience high-risk withdrawal symptoms, including seizures and Delirium Tremens. Illicit drinkers across the community experience incredibly high rates of alcohol and drug-related harm including but not limited to drug poisoning death, accidental injury, and interactions with law enforcement, and hepatic injury. Over 80% of [ORG] members are Indigenous, and experience high rates of alcohol-associated harm intersecting with socioeconomic marginality and intergenerational experience of colonial violence. card_text: Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health, regarding its study on the ‘Opioid Epidemic and Toxic Drug Crisis’ January 2024 I. Introduction The [ORG] promotes the human rights of people living with HIV or AIDS and other populations disproportionately affected by HIV, punitive laws and policies, and criminalization, in Canada and internationally. For three decades, we have advocated for drug policy grounded in evidence, human rights, and public health. The [ORG] works collaboratively with governments, affected communities, and civil society to improve community health and safety by conducting research and outreach on effective and evidence-based policy responses to substance use. We are grateful for the opportunity to make this joint submission detailing key legal determinants of health that have spurred the toxic drug crisis. II. Criminalization and Canada’s Drug Poisoning Crisis Canada’s approach to drugs, focused primarily on prohibition rather than prevention, harm reduction, and support for people who use drugs, has done catastrophic harm, fuelling deadly stigma, racism, epidemics of preventable illness and death, and widespread, egregious violations of human rights. Criminalizing drugs pushes people to use their drugs in isolation, compromises their ability to take vital safety precautions, and deters people from essential health care and harm reduction services.i Research has shown frequent contact police have with people who use drugs contributes to their “health risk environment through pathways, such as syringe and naloxone confiscation, and physical and verbal harassment” which can lead to syringe sharing, rushed injection, and isolation while using drugs.ii Furthermore, studies

Name: BRIEF RECOMMENDATIONS TO THE HOUSE OF COMMONS STANDING COMMITTEE ON HEALTH ON THE OPIOID EPIDEMIC
study_title: Opioid Epidemic and Toxic Drug Crisis in Canada
card_text: BRIEF RECOMMENDATIONS TO THE HOUSE OF COMMONS STANDING COMMITTEE ON HEALTH ON THE OPIOID EPIDEMIC AND TOXIC DRUG CRISIS IN CANADA Amber Streukens January 15, 2024 This brief is submitted on behalf of the Rural Empowered Drug Users Network (REDUN), a grassroots support, education, and advocacy group of people with lived and living experience (PWLLE) of substance use meeting in the West Kootenay region of BC. With organizational support from [ORG], a longstanding leader in rural harm reduction - REDUN hosts biweekly meetings in 3 communities, provides peer led harm reduction services, and advocates for the needs of rural PWLLE. Despite high rates of injury and fatality in rural regions (estimated at 48.5/100,000 in the Kootenay Boundary HSDA in 2023i), access to harm reduction and recovery services is still extremely limited outside of urban centers. People who use substances (PWUS) in the Kootenay region have inadequate access to: opioid agonist therapy (OAT), pharmaceutical alternatives to the toxic drug supply, drug checking, overdose prevention services, withdrawal management, inpatient treatment, recovery services, supportive housing, relapse prevention, and supportive housing. No community is untouched by this crisis. It is imperative that the federal government take immediate, concrete action to reduce harms across the country. Approaching elections, it seems this issue has moved from the realm of public health into political theater. This is unacceptably dehumanizing and poses a serious threat to human life. Ideology has no place in emergency public health response. Individuals and communities in crisis must not be sacrificed for political

Name: “Innovating Beyond Exclusively Medicalized Approaches” Policy Brief and Recommendations Updated February 2023
org: Canadian Civil Society Advancing Safe Supply Working Group
study_title: Opioid Epidemic and Toxic Drug Crisis in Canada
card_text: “Innovating Beyond Exclusively Medicalized Approaches” Policy Brief and Recommendations Updated February 2023 Executive Summary • Amidst a nation-wide illegal drug toxicity1 crisis, pharmaceutical-grade alternatives to the illegal drug supply (referred to henceforth as “safe supply”) are an effective intervention for mitigating drug policy- related harms. • Medicalized models of safe supply produce positive impacts for some people who use drugs (PWUD) and the broader public. • Individual benefits for PWUD participating in safe supply include reductions in illegal drug use, overdose, and criminalized behaviour, and increased positive health and housing outcomes. • Community benefits of safe supply include reduction in health care utilization and associated health care system costs, among others. • However, the reach of existing prescriber-driven, or medicalized, safe supply programs is highly limited; as such, they are not an adequate standalone response to the urgent drug toxicity crisis. Medicalized approaches also present barriers and exclusions for racialized and other marginalized populations. • Precariously funded pilot studies are impermanent and unsustainable. • Medicalized safe supply programs are mired in capacity, institutional, and guideline constraints, accommodating only a fraction of the hundreds of thousands who would benefit from legal access to a pharmaceutical-grade supply of drugs. • The requirement that one be labeled with a “substance use disorder” to access medicalized safe supply exacerbates drug-related stigma. • Medicalized safe supply is not available to those who use episodically, despite this cohort being at the highest risk for drug poisoning given their lack of drug tolerance. • Medicalized safe supply

card_text: Memorandum – Nathalie Vaillant: If I Had Known Submitted to: Standing Committee on Health (SCH) Subject: Monitoring of Medical Devices (Breast Implants) 30 April 2023 PREAMBULE Quebec’s motto is “Je me souviens” . . . well, mine is: IF I HAD KNOWN INTRODUCTION This memorandum is being submitted without a presentation and will describe all the struggles I have had that could have been avoided . . . IF I HAD KNOWN, IF I HAD BEEN CLEARLY INFORMED. If you’re in good health, why be afraid of the word cancer? In your family, you have one sister who had breast cancer very young; you’re not immune. You perform your breast self-exam and find a small lump. You tell yourself, “Nah, not me, that can’t be!” When you see your treating physician and ask for every test imaginable to rule out any likelihood of bad news and she tells you, “Just because your sister had cancer doesn’t mean you’ll get it.” So again, I am asking the question: Why be afraid of the word cancer? When you read my history, you will notice that all the surgeries I’ve undergone are in red, to highlight the importance of monitoring medical devices. I had bouts of mild fever and night sweats. I would wake up at night, soaked in sweat and shivering like a leaf. I had lost my spark. I oŌen felt listless. I had become someone I didn’t recognize. I was losing a lot of hair. We had been trying to have our litle girl for five years, but I couldn’t get pregnant. My periods were irregular. I consulted an internist. I felt like I was at death’s door. I felt a desperate need to stay alive. I felt old. I really felt old for my age. I stopped smoking (so much the beter) because I kept having a recurring fear of death. At last, I got pregnant, by some miracle, and our daughter was born. She came out limp as a rag doll. I was so scared for her! But I had a whole team behind me to get through this ordeal. She’s doing well now. What a relief! But my own health has goten much worse. By 2021, I could hardly get through the day. Fortunately, when my maternity leave ended, I was on telework. In 2022, we travelled to Maine on vacation and I got sick. card_text: Dominique Lévesque 42 years old Implants: Smooth round moderate profile gel breast implant cohesive 300cc #350-7300BC Batch: 5629023 and 5653215 Implanted on 2006-05-31 Explanted on 2023-03-28 Hello, I had Mentor smooth silicone implants 300cc from May 31, 2006, to March 28, 2023. I was 26 years old at the time of the operation and I had several health problems following this implantation. Like many women in my situation, I have experienced health problems throughout my almost 17 years of living with my implants. This exhaustive list is minimal compared to some of us. Here are some of the most striking symptoms: chronic fatigue for the first two years, inflammation, joint problems, muscle pain, night sweats from the age of 28-30, gastrointestinal problems, liver problems, allergies and food intolerances that developed without warning, repeated sinusitis. All of these problems exist otherwise, but here they only have one thing in common: all women with breast implants notice these symptoms. Is this a coincidence? Of course not! Knowing all this now, I can only hope that Health Canada can properly advise women who will have to choose to have breast implants in their lives. These toxic sacks poison more women than we think and some manufacturers do not follow the evolution of their product over time to see all these problems that are closely related to it. Name: Saint-Colomban, May 4, 2023 To whom it may concern: In 2007, at the age of 29, I thought I had made the right decision. I had a breast augmentation that I thought was safe. Despite the kindness of the
card_text: Saint-Colomban, May 4, 2023 To whom it may concern: In 2007, at the age of 29, I thought I had made the right decision. I had a breast augmentation that I thought was safe. Despite the kindness of the surgeon, Dr. Jacques Papillon, we were misled to believe that the implants were safe. If a registry had existed, I would have made a better choice for my health. Had there been a registry, thousands of women would have been contacted in one way or another more officially than through a support group. Why does Canada approve these implants and then turn a blind eye? Enough is enough! It is high time for this to change. It is Health Canada’s responsibility to implement a medical implant registry that would allow patients to be traced and notified immediately if problems arise. Automakers have a registry—good on them! France has a registry—bravo! ...So why not us? I never would have gone through this if I had known. Women must have the right to know the risks and impacts on their health. About three years after the augmentation, I noticed abnormal hair loss that got worse over the years. Other symptoms appeared, like fatigue, and sometimes I had to take two naps during the day. I’ve also experienced dry eyes and joint problems. Thanks to the support group, I realized that I was far from alone and that my case was not the worst. I decided to have my implants removed after 15 years

Name: Dear Members of Parliament,
card_text: Dear Members of Parliament, I am writing to you to share my personal experience with having textured Mentor breast implants for 14 years in hopes this may urge you to consider establishing a Breast Implant Registry in Canada. I hope that my story will serve as a testimony to the need for a comprehensive database to track breast implant-related complications and adverse events. Within 6 months of implantation, I developed questionable lumps that required monitoring every 6 months via mammograms and ultrasounds. As time went on, I started experiencing digestive issues, ongoing rashes, vision issues, headaches, neck and shoulder pain, intense brain fog, chest pains, and difficulty breathing. These symptoms also caused multiple bouts of depression. Despite visiting multiple doctors and specialists and being told that everything was fine and perhaps all was stress related, I finally felt that my underlying issues were finally validated after receiving a letter from my implanting surgeon outlining the symptoms of Breast Implant Illness (BII) associated with my breast implants. My immediate reaction was to make an appointment with him to discuss the letter and have the implants removed. Unfortunately, my visit was unwelcome, and I was dismissed as the letter was only sent out of obligation and not because of his belief in BII. He told me that I would look disfigured by removing them and that he honestly did not believe they were causing my issues. Foolishly, I followed his advice and left the implants in. As time passed, my symptoms worsened

card_text: Good morning to all members of the HESA Committee, After observing the meetings of the past weeks, I felt compelled to write to all of you. Thank you for giving us a voice and a platform to bring forth our concerns. My name is Dawn Criss, and I am a BIA ALCL survivor, a BII patient and a member of the advocacy team alongside Ms. McGregor, Ms. Elliott and Ms. Pratt. I was the first BIA ALCL patient diagnosed in Alberta in 2018 and I am also a patient of Dr. Tervaert. It seems widely agreed upon, that an implant registry is long overdue. However, had we incorporated a registry when implants were first introduced to the market 60 years ago, we would have had enough data to contact patients with recalls of defective products, recalls for BIA ALCL and other cancers and also to pull data for our own Canadian studies. To start from scratch now, it will take many years to gather what’s required to accomplish any of these tasks. Perhaps we could find a way for patients AND plastic surgeons to add previous surgical/pathological data and adverse reaction events into the new registry, allowing us to grow the database much quicker? This registry should be an "opt out" database similar to an excel spreadsheet, where all information can eventually be sorted and filtered to pull data for any study required. I found the best surgeon in Montreal, and this doctor performed my cosmetic surgery from 1983 to 2015. The first surgery went well; I was still quite young, and these were my first implants. I remember going back to see the surgeon three months after surgery because the left side was hard and painful. A shell had formed, and this was not good. The doctor broke and crushed the shell with both hands, and he was satisfied. It was very painful, but the shell did not form again. At the time, I was told that this was a normal complication. I gave birth to two girls in 1994 and 1995 at the age of 32 and 34, respectively. I breastfed my babies while the old implants were still in place. Name: House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Brief Concerning the Oversight of Medical Devices (Breast Implants) Introduction
card_text: House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Brief Concerning the Oversight of Medical Devices (Breast Implants) Introduction I would like to thank the members of the Committee for the invitation to submit this brief so I can provide my views regarding the oversight of breast implants. Evolution of breast implants Between January 1992 and October 20, 2006, Health Canada imposed a moratorium on silicone implants to protect the health of all Canadian women and to ensure that these products are safe. These implants were withdrawn from the Canadian market due to safety concerns and the fact that they cause serious health problems for women, including cancer, connective tissue diseases and autoimmune diseases. Thirty years later, we are still having the same debates, the same health challenges and the same doubts vis-à-vis the safety of these products. Anaplastic large cell lymphoma cases are trending higher, as are incidences of other breast implant-related diseases. The time for oversight has passed—it is now time for action. Stronger oversight measures, in the form of a medical devices registry, should have been put in place at the appropriate time, when the moratorium on silicone breast implants was imposed in 1992. This would have made it possible to track all breast implants and maximize oversight for Canadian women. The moratorium and the Special Access Program Lack of a “breast implant registry” resulted in ineffective oversight, prevention and safety considerations. Even though Health Canada did not approve these prostheses during the moratorium, it did provide Canadian surgeons

card_text: A Submission on the Oversight of Medical Devices (Breast Implants) to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Prepared by Terri McGregor, Expert BIA-ALCL Advocate | May 2023 HESA Submission – Oversight of Medical Devices (Breast Implants) The [ORG] ([ORG]) is an organization of patient advocates and knowledge experts that work with patients and leaders in Canada, the US and all over the world. With limited time and resources, this brief was prepared by Terri McGregor, a BIA-ALCL survivor Expert BIA-ALCL Patient Advocate and part of the [ORG] core leadership team as a result of collaborative efforts with members of [ORG] including Canadian Representative Julie Elliott and Canadian Patient Advocate Nancy Pratt, founder of the BIFI Society of Canada and co-founder of the Canadian Breast Implant Failure and Illness support group. Breast implants are not life saving devices and thus there should be a very low tolerance for any risk to health, especially risks that impose huge costs on the healthcare system. There is no informed consent without data. Healthcare delivery and costs cannot be optimized without data. We appreciate that the House of Common Standing Committee on Health is taking steps to examine the feasibility of creating a nationwide registry to advance our common goal of patient safety. Name: A Submission on the Oversight of Medical Devices (Breast Implants) to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health May 2023
card_text: A Submission on the Oversight of Medical Devices (Breast Implants) to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health May 2023 Oversight of Medical Devices (Breast Implants) [ORG] is grateful for the opportunity to provide its recommendations on the oversight of breast implants. [ORG] is a neutral, not-for profit organization that creates and maintains global supply chain standards in collaboration with GS1 Member Organizations in 116 countries (we are often best known for the global barcode standard). Through a federated model, collectively, we help millions of companies around the world use common identifiers on their products, locations, and services, to enable traceability and recalls, inventory management, and visibility within the supply chain. The GS1 global supply chain standards already play a significant role in the oversight of breast implants, globally. Due to regulatory requirements from other jurisdictions such the US and EU, the majority of breast implants that come to, or are made-in, Canada, already have GS1 identifiers (ie: a global barcode) on its product. This acts as a unique device identifier (UDI) for the product that is recognized globally. Therefore, Canada is already starting with a strong foundation for proper oversight of breast implants including for traceability capabilities, recalls and a data-rich registry critical for research and analytics. We are pleased to be currently engaged with the Medical Devices Directorate within Health Canada to support in the development of an optimal UDI policy for Canada. Name: A Submission on the Oversight of Medical Devices (Breast Implants) to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health January 2023
card_text: A Submission on the Oversight of Medical Devices (Breast Implants) to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health January 2023 HESA Submission – Oversight of medical devices (breast implants) The [ORG] ([ORG]) is the only national charity supporting Canadians with all cancers in communities across the country. As the voice for Canadians who care about cancer, we are pleased to share with members of the Standing Committee on Health background information on breast cancer in Canada and how breast implants play a role in the recovery of people living with breast cancer. It is estimated that about 1 in 8 Canadian women will develop breast cancer during their lifetime and 1 in 34 will die from it.1 Overview of breast cancer in Canada It is estimated that in 2022: 28,600 Canadian women will be diagnosed with breast cancer. This represents 25% of all new cancer cases in women in 2022. 5,500 Canadian women will die from breast cancer. This represents 14% of all cancer deaths in women in 2022. On average, 78 Canadian women will be diagnosed with breast cancer every day. On average, 15 Canadian women will die from breast cancer every day. 270 Canadian men will be diagnosed with breast cancer and 55 will die from breast cancer.1 Role of breast reconstruction and prostheses If needed as part of treatment plan, surgery for breast cancer may include breastconserving surgery (lumpectomy) or mastectomy (removes all of the breast). Name: Submission to the Standing Committee on Health’s study of the Patented Medicine Prices Review Board Submitted by [ORG] (formerly Multiple Sclerosis Society of Canada) Introduction
card_text: Submission to the Standing Committee on Health’s study of the Patented Medicine Prices Review Board Submitted by [ORG] (formerly Multiple Sclerosis Society of Canada) Introduction [ORG], formerly the Multiple Sclerosis Society of Canada, is pleased to provide this submission to the Standing Committee on Health’s study of the Patented Medicine Prices Review Board (PMPRB). MS impacts all Canadians – not only individuals living with the disease, but also their families. The unpredictable, episodic, and progressive nature of MS makes it significantly challenging for many people to maintain a similar quality of life they had prior to diagnosis. For Canadians living with MS, timely and affordable access to disease-modifying therapies (DMTs) is vital to increase their quality of life by delaying disability progression and improving health outcomes. As Canada emerges from the pandemic amidst a significantly strained healthcare system, Canadians living with MS are faced with additional challenges, including barriers to accessing life-altering DMTs. Over the past six years, [ORG] has sent eight submissions in response to the PMPRB’s proposed amendments. Our position has consistently been that any amendments must take into consideration the impact on people living with MS, as we postulate that PMPRB amendments could hinder affordability and availability of disease-modifying therapies (DMTs) for people living with MS, as well as research and clinical trials for future medications. We question whether the voice of patient advocacy groups like [ORG] were given full consideration over the past six years, as concerns about the impact of changes on availability and investment

Name: June 14, 2023
card_text: June 14, 2023 Standing Committee on Health Sixth Floor, [CONTACT] House of Commons Ottawa ON [CONTACT] Subject: Study on the Patented Medicine Prices Review Board (PMPRB) Reforms Dear Honourable Members of the Standing Committee on Health, The [ORG] ([ORG]) is the national alliance of over 100 rare disease patient organizations and representing nearly 3 million Canadians affected by rare diseases. From the outset of the PMPRB reform process, our position has been clear. we support improving affordability of rare disease medicines while simultaneously assuring appropriate and timely access. However, the changes proposed by PMPRB, starting in 2017, have been singularly focused on one objective, that is, driving prices as low as possible, going far beyond even their original mandate of assuring prices were not excessive as an abuse of patent and in comparison to other countries. This approach would have had a devastating impact on Canadians living with rare diseases, not only delaying access to new medicines but actually denying their availability to Canadian patients, starting with the decline or delay of clinical trials taking place in Canadian sites. In this context, [ORG] and other stakeholders have raised numerous concerns and put forward thoughtful alternative proposals for how to address the federal government’s medicine affordability objectives without harming the access environment for new medicines. In too many cases, these concerns have largely fallen on deaf ears. We have consistently warned that the PMPRB’s pricing framework would unduly and unreasonably limit access to life-saving and life-improving medicines for patients with rare

Name: Office of the Chief Executive Officer Government of Canada House of Commons Submission Brief
card_text: Office of the Chief Executive Officer Government of Canada House of Commons Submission Brief Study on Patented Medicine Prices Review Board [ORG] April 28, 2023 Submitted to Standing Committee on Health (HESA) The [ORG] (Neighbourhood Pharmacies) represents leading pharmacy organizations across the country, including chain, banner, long-term care, grocery chains, specialty pharmacies, and mass merchandisers with pharmacies across Canada. We advance the delivery of care through over 11,500 pharmacies and their teams, that serve as integral community health hubs in urban, suburban, rural, remote and First Nations neighbourhoods.1 As the association representing pharmacy operators across the country, we act as a panCanadian level to support policy makers with the development of innovative solutions that allow pharmacies to support primary care while advocating for fair and sustainable funding for community pharmacies. We are providing this submission to the Standing Committee on Health as part of its study of the Patented Medicine Prices Review Board (PMPRB), on behalf of our member pharmacies across the country. Specifically, we recommend a ‘whole of government approach’ to pharmaceutical pricing policy decisions that: • Recognizes the downstream impact of multiple pharmaceutical price compression policies on Canadians’ access to medications; • Reviews the mandate and oversight structures of the PMPRB to ensure that protecting Canadians from punitive medication prices does not result in loss of access to these medications; and • Involves broader stakeholder inclusion from across the entire patient care pathway in any further PMPRB initiatives. Name: Submission to the Standing Committee on Health Study on the Treatment and Prevention of Cancer
card_text: Submission to the Standing Committee on Health Study on the Treatment and Prevention of Cancer December 9, 2024 Introduction The [ORG] ([ORG]) represents more than 3 million workers, bringing together Canada’s national and international unions, along with provincial and territorial Federations of Labour and more than 100 district Labour Councils. Our members work in practically all sectors of the economy, in all occupations, in all parts of Canada. We are pleased to offer our position as it pertains to the treatment and prevention of cancer. Regardless of the approach taken by government, we submit that it should embody the determinants of health, and account for the realities and needs of equity-deserving groups, including women, racialized and Indigenous workers, and workers with disabilities. An Ounce of Prevention and the Right to Know The right of workers to know of hazards in their workplace is fundamental. The [ORG] has long taken the position that an “ounce of prevention is worth a pound of cure” with respect to cancer, including those that are acquired on the job (occupational cancer). To this end, the fundamental right to know of hazards in the workplace must be adhered to, not only for known carcinogens, but also, employing the precautionary principle, for potential carcinogens. As such, all chemicals must be assessed utilizing a hazardsbased approach utilizing the hierarchy of controls; this means that, wherever possible, known or potentially carcinogenic chemicals must be avoided. Name: Submission to House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Study on: Treatment and Prevention of Cancer November 2024
org: Advocacy for Canadian Childhood Oncology Research Network
card_text: Submission to House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Study on: Treatment and Prevention of Cancer November 2024 www.ac2orn.com Keith McIntosh Ac2orn ([ORG]), is pleased to provide the following comments to support the Committee’s study on the treatment and prevention of cancer in Canada. It is important for the Committee to allocate specific time to hear and consider the distinct issues faced by children and young people with cancer. Children are not small adults, and there are unique challenges to treating and conducting research on childhood, adolescent, and young adult cancers. When a young person is diagnosed with cancer it has profound long-term physical, emotional, and financial impacts on the individual and their family. Ac2orn was founded in 2014 to advocate for translational research and effective treatments to support the goal of curing childhood, adolescent, and young adult (CAYA) cancers. It is made up of parents from across Canada, who have children with different types of cancer, and the desire to help improve pediatric oncology research and outcomes. Ac2orn works to achieve its mission by connecting with CAYA health advocacy groups across Canada; identifying and addressing barriers that exist in accessing treatments and cancer clinical trials, and educating policy makers, industry, and others about the issues facing the CAYA cancer community. Name: November 1, 2024 To: House of Commons, Standing Committee on Health (HESA) via [CONTACT] From: [ORG]
card_text: November 1, 2024 To: House of Commons, Standing Committee on Health (HESA) via [CONTACT] From: [ORG] RE: The HESA study on Cancer Treatment and Prevention Subject: Urgent need for a dedicated HESA study reviewing Health Canada’s exposure limits for radiofrequency frequencies now used for 5G telecommunication and information technologies. Lack of evidence is not proof of safety. ---------------------- 5G wireless technologies such as smart phones, tablets, virtual headsets and wearables are being marketed and purchased across Canada, with enthusiasm. Millions of Canadians are being exposed to the non-ionizing radiofrequency electromagnetic fields that these devices emit in order to work. What most don’t realize is that the emissions from these technologies have not been safety-tested for health outcomes such as cancer. How do we know this? It is in a Health Canada 5G report that is available to the public upon request. In 2011, radiofrequency radiation, such as emitted by cell phones, Bluetooth, Wi-Fi and 5G technologies, was classified as a Group 2B, possible carcinogen, by the World Health Organization’s International Agency for Research on Cancer.1 IARC then noted particularly the need for studies in animals. In 2018, the U.S. National Toxicology Program concluded from its multimillion-dollar animal studies, 2 that cell phone type exposures, conducted under non-heating (non-thermal) conditions, caused cancer.3 Later in 2018, the Ramazzini Institute in Italy published similar findings for cell tower level exposures.4 This and subsequent evidence from human, animal and tissue studies, published in peer-reviewed journals, support reclassification to a Group 1 known human carcinogen,5

Name: Shelley Wright Director, [ORG] [CONTACT] Cancer Prevention
card_text: Shelley Wright Director, [ORG] [CONTACT] Cancer Prevention Submission to Parliament of Canada House of Commons Standing Committee on Health (HESA), RF-EMR is an Environmental co-factor of Childhood Cancers Oct. 12, 2024 The national non-profit [ORG] ([ORG]) respectfully submits, there is mounting evidence and concern that radiofrequency electromagnetic radiation (RF-EMR) represents a serious environmental contributor to cancer requiring recognition, rigorous independent assessment, and public health attention to encourage families to practice safe tech when using wireless (e.g., RF-EMR microwave frequency) technology. I’m a retired teacher and Director of [ORG]. We work with families of children, and teachers, injured by wireless RF-EMR. Children and school staff currently suffer chronic or debilitating adverse effects, including cancers, caused by continuous and cumulative RF-EMR in schools, playgrounds, and daycares. It is unnecessary to expose children to harmful radiation; safer wired technologies exist. Cancer prevention is far more effective and humane, and less costly for children, families, and society than treatment. While early detection has value, reduction of toxic exposures at homes and school environments should be paramount. We are deeply concerned that the escalating use of wireless devices and infrastructure (cell towers and antennas) intensifies RF-EMR exposures in educational settings. A substantial body of scientific evidence shows RF-EMR can cause biological effects linked to serious health issues, including cancers. Although the World Health Organization’s International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC) classified RF radiation as a possible carcinogen (Group 2B) in 2011, a cancer epidemiology update demonstrates a clearer link between electromagnetic radiation and cancer.1

Name: Access to Gamma Knife in Canada Study: Treatment and Prevention of Cancer Brief to the Standing Committee on Health November 3, 2024
card_text: Access to Gamma Knife in Canada Study: Treatment and Prevention of Cancer Brief to the Standing Committee on Health November 3, 2024 Ioana Grigorescu November 3, 2024 Brief to the Standing Committee on Health Study: Treatment and Prevention of Cancer Dear Members of the Standing Committee on Health, Thank you for conducting this study. This brief refers to decades of inequity in Canada regarding access to highly specialized brain radiation treatments. Depending on their postal code, Canadians are offered or denied access to specialized brain radiation. In 2002, Canadian Premiers agreed on equitable access to specialized treatments performed in centres of excellence. BC Standing Committee on Health issued the report “Patients First 2002: The Path to Reform” (1). It states: Sites of Excellence For High Risk - Low Volume Surgery In 2002, the Premiers of the provinces agreed to cooperate on consolidating high-risk, low volume surgery in a few designated centres across the country. The decision came from increasing research evidence, including the coroner inquest into the deaths of 12 children in Winnipeg’s pediatric cardiac surgery program, that large population densities are needed to safely deliver rare, high-risk surgery. Pediatric cardiac surgery, rarer organ transplants, and gamma knife radiation neurosurgery are examples of the advanced techniques that require specialized equipment and a highly experienced and trained team of health care providers. They should therefore be concentrated in a few centres in Canada. Details of the Sites of Excellence for low volume surgery are still being worked out. B.C. will likely

Name: October 27, 2024 By email: [CONTACT] From: Frank Clegg CEO, [ORG] ([ORG])
card_text: October 27, 2024 By email: [CONTACT] From: Frank Clegg CEO, [ORG] ([ORG]) [CONTACT] Subject: Recommendations to HESA regarding “Cancer Treatment and Prevention” 1) Support updating Health Canada’s 2015 guidelines for radiofrequency/microwave electromagnetic radiation, to truly protect public health. 2) Support full implementation of HESA 2015 recommendations relating to cancer and radiofrequency radiation. 3) Encourage innovations for increased access to wired information technologies, and for safer wireless technologies. 4) Encourage best practices: As Low As Reasonably Achievable (ALARA) emissions 5) Support a follow-up by HESA of the 2015 HESA study on the effects of radiofrequency electromagnetic radiation and the health of Canadians. 6) Support Investigations of some radiofrequencies as an adjuvant treatment for cancer. I write as the Chief Executive Officer of [ORG] ([ORG]), a national, non-partisan, not-for-profit organization concerned with the health risks of wireless technology. We are a volunteer coalition of parents, scientists and citizens whose mission is to educate Canadians and policy makers about the dangers of exposures to unsafe levels of radiofrequency/microwave radiation from commonly used wireless devices such as cell phones and telecommunications (cell) tower antennas. I also serve on the board of the Environmental Health Trust (EHTrust). Dr. Devra Davis, founder and former head of EHTrust has many outstanding credentials such as being the founding director, Center for Environmental Oncology, University of Pittsburgh Cancer Institute, as well as the founding director of the Board on Environmental Studies and Toxicology of the U.S. National Research Council, National Academy of Sciences. https://ehtrust.org/about/dr-devra-davis/ [ORG] is a strong advocate

Name: Cancer treatment and prevention Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health (HESA) September 13, 2024 - updated: October 8, 2024 [ORG] ([ORG]) respectfully submits that primary can
card_text: Cancer treatment and prevention Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health (HESA) September 13, 2024 - updated: October 8, 2024 [ORG] ([ORG]) respectfully submits that primary cancer prevention, addressing root contributors to the disease process, merits the highest priority, to prevent cancer before it starts. Screening is secondary prevention, hopefully averting the worst with earlier treatment. We are scientists, medical experts and other volunteers, focusing diverse knowledge of environmental health, medicine, chemistry and scientific approaches on cancer. Here, we summarize why and how to “make least-toxic the norm” for cancer prevention. When [ORG] was established in 2007, heart disease was Canada’s #1 cause of death. Today, more Canadians die from cancer than any other single cause, and aggressive cancers are developing everearlier in life. Costs associated with screening, diagnoses, treatment and follow-up are unsustainable; lost wages are substantial; and patients’ and families’ pain and losses must not be under-estimated. Cancer trends We join many scientists stressing urgency to identify and address underlying cancer risk factors. While long-term Canadian data is lacking, we know that similar to American data, cancer diagnoses and deaths are increasing, particularly at younger ages.1,2 Cancer incidence is also increasing in younger Canadians,3 with the highest rates among White and Indigenous populations. • Liver and intrahepatic bile duct cancer, kidney cancer, melanoma and non-Hodgkin lymphoma are increasing in younger Canadians.4 • Colorectal cancer has increased in those 20 to 44 years of age.5 Recently, with colorectal cancer rates increasing in both females and males

Name: [ORG]’s Recommendations to the Standing Committee on Health on the Study on Prevention and Treatment of Cancer Cancer remains the leading cause of death in Canada. An estimated 2 in 5 people living in
card_text: [ORG]’s Recommendations to the Standing Committee on Health on the Study on Prevention and Treatment of Cancer Cancer remains the leading cause of death in Canada. An estimated 2 in 5 people living in Canada will be diagnosed with cancer in their lifetime and about 1 in 4 will die from cancer. In 2024, 247,100 people living in Canada are estimated to be diagnosed.1 With an ageing population, the number of people diagnosed with cancer and cancer mortality is projected to rise significantly in the coming decades. While our health care system is pushed to the brink, people living with cancer continue to face barriers at every step of their cancer journey. The reality is people in Canada living with cancer are dying while they wait for care. Many are faced with the impossible choice of putting food on the table or taking on significant debt as they struggle to afford the out-of-pocket costs associated with a cancer diagnosis, such as, drugs and treatments, medical transportation and accommodation and loss of pay due to inability to work, among other costs. For underserved and equity-deserving populations in Canada, these challenges are even greater. CANA’s recommendations are intended to guide action that will promote better health and better outcomes for all people living with cancer in Canada. We urge the Standing Committee on Health to consider the issues presented here and incorporate our recommendations during your discussion on the study on the treatment and prevention of cancer. Name: Hypothesis on the Global Pandemic of the Early Onset of Cancers Michael Gilbertson, PhD Brief to the Parliament of Canada House of Commons Health Committee 3 September, 2024
card_text: Hypothesis on the Global Pandemic of the Early Onset of Cancers Michael Gilbertson, PhD Brief to the Parliament of Canada House of Commons Health Committee 3 September, 2024 I am Michael Gilbertson and I am an independent researcher. I have a Doctor of Philosophy degree in occupational and environmental health from the University of Stirling, in Scotland. In 2004, I retired after thirty-four years with the Government of Canada. In the 1970s, I was part of the organization charged with implementing the Environmental Contaminants Act (1975) and I worked as the Early Warning Coordinator identifying sentinel biological events from exposures to chemicals in the environment. My final years of service were with the International Joint Commission where I specialized in inferring the causal relations between the biological effects observed in fish, wildlife and human populations exposed to persistent toxic chemicals in Great Lakes food webs. Following my retirement from public service, I co-chaired the Halifax Project, an initiative involving 350 cancer scientists from 31 countries, half of whom were focused on the potential role of low-dose, biologically disruptive, common environmental chemicals that might be acting in concert with one another and contributing to various aspects of carcinogenesis. I was a co-author in this project and the results of the work were published in a series of articles in a special issue of Carcinogenesis, a journal of Oxford University Press, and which was also discussed in Environmental Health Perspectives, another peer reviewed journal with a high impact factor. card_text: Stakeholder Brief Adapted for The House of Commons Standing Committee on Health (HESA) Women’s Health 44th Parliament, 1st Session Meeting 108 Session Montreal, April 13th, 2023 OBJECT: Proactive Family-Centered and Trauma-Informed Care in the Perinatal Mental Health Dear Decision Maker, Stakeholder, or Partner, The following partners have collaborated in the writing process of this brief: The Quebec Alliance for Perinatal Mental Health (AQSMP), Principal Investigators from the [ORG] ([ORG]), [ORG] ([ORG]), The Network of Perinatal Resource Centers of Quebec (RCRPQ), The Olo Foundation, The Quebec Association of Midwives. As organizations and professionals working with and on behalf of Quebec residents during the perinatal period, we would like to present you with some recommendations and concrete actions to care for families during the perinatal period in Quebec and Canada. Please note that these recommendations are the result of exchanges among the cosignatories. Support is needed from the federal and provincial governments to ensure that provincial stakeholders, led by alliances such as ours, can: 1) work towards the recognition of perinatal mental health care as a priority medical activity throughout the healthcare network; 2) better structure the political actions of the Quebec and other provincial alliances as they work within the entire healthcare network; 3) establish expert committees for certain aspects such as the prevention of perinatal mental health problems; 4) establishing a timeline for changes to be made over the next 4 years. card_text: Submission to the House of Commons Health Committee Study on Women’s Health Prioritizing Women’s Health Research Will Improve Health For All Submitted by Laura Gravelsins, Tallinn Splinter and Liisa Galea on behalf of Women’s Health Research Cluster and womenmindTM at the [ORG]’s April 29, 2024 Websites: https://www.galealab.com/ https://womenshealthresearch.ubc.ca/ https://www.camh.ca/en/get-involved/join-the-cause/womenmind Women’s health research has been undervalued, understudied, and underfunded for decades, which leads to disparities in health for everyone 1,2. Although human females live longer than human males, they are also more likely to live with chronic disease, and a recent world economic forum report suggested that females spend 25% more time across their lifetime in poorer health compared to males3. For many disorders, there are sex differences in prevalence, as women are twice more likely to be diagnosed with depression and Alzheimer’s disease than men4. Not only are sport concussions twice more likely to occur in women than in men, women show poorer outcomes than men5. Women have a unique physiology and experiences that impact their health differently compared to men, and many mental health conditions affect women differently and at different times in their lives than men6. Indeed, the time of greatest risk for first-time depression in a female’s lifetime is during the postpartum 7,8. And yet, despite these known differences we found that only 3% of over 3000 neuroscience and psychiatry studies considered women’s health variables questions 9. card_text: [CONTACT] – Unit 5 London ON [CONTACT] t. [CONTACT] f. [CONTACT] [CONTACT] u Providing counselling, advocacy, and support for abused women. Brief to the Standing Committee on Health Submitted by: Jennifer Dunn, Executive Director, London Abused Women’s Centre (Canada) April 15, 2024 Chair and Members, Standing Committee on Health: The London Abused Women’s Centre (LAWC) is a feminist organization that supports and advocates for personal, social, and systemic change directed at ending male violence against women and girls. LAWC is a non-residential agency that provides women and girls over the age of 12 who have been subjected to abuse, assault, sexual exploitation, trafficking, and non-state torture with immediate access to long-term, traumainformed counselling, advocacy, and support. In 2023, LAWC had over 11,000 service interactions. Of those 11,000, over 4,000 individual counselling, group and urgent support service interactions. This includes approximately 400 women and girls who reported being trafficked or were at risk of being trafficked. This also includes 112 women who reported being subjected to non-state torture. During this same time, LAWC also answered over 7,000 calls for support. It is impossible to study women’s health without looking through the lens of male violence against women and inequality. Despite strides toward ending male violence, women continue to navigate through societal structures that perpetuate their vulnerability. This brief explains the dimensions of women's health based on the work we do at the London Abused Women’s Centre which we are hoping will “Shine the Light”1 on the urgent need for comprehensive support

card_text: Improving Black Women’s Health in Canada Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Study on Women’s Health By Professor Bukola Salami, Tier 1 Canada Research Chair in Black and Racialized People’s Health, Department of Community Health Sciences, University of Calgary Craig Wellington, Chief Executive Officer, Black Opportunity Fund Jeanne Lehman, Executive Director, Black Women in Action Dr. Modupe Tunde-Byass, Obstetrician and Gynecologist, North York General Hospital; Associate Professor, Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology, University of Toronto Paul Bailey, Executive Director, Black Health Alliance Professor Andre Renzaho, Distinguished Professor, Translational Health Research Institute, Western Sydney University Dr. Ato Sekyi-Otu, Orthopedic Surgeon, William Osler Health System; Lecturer, Department of Surgery, University of Toronto Mary Olukotun, PhD Student, Faculty of Nursing, University of Alberta Dr. Regine King, Associate Professor, Faculty of Social Work, University of Calgary Dr. Oluwakemi Amodu, Assistant Professor, Faculty of Nursing, University of Alberta Professor Solina Richter, Dean, College of Nursing, University of Saskatchewan Dr. Tehseen Ladha, Assistant Professor, Faculty of Medicine and Dentistry, University of Alberta Dr. Maria Beatriz Ospina, PhD, Associate Professor, Department of Public Health Sciences, Queen’s University April 2024 Black people are a growing population in Canada. Between 2016 and 2021, the Black population in Canada grew from 3.5% to 4.3% of the total Canadian population (Statistics Canada, 2023). As of 2021, Black people comprised 16.1% of the racialized population in Canada, with approximately 1.5 million people identifying as Black. Black women experience poorer economic and social outcomes than the general Canadian population.

Name: Brief to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Prepared by: Dr. Ryan Van Lieshout, MD, PhD, FRCPC ([CONTACT]) Associate Professor, Department of Psychiatry and Behavioural Neurosciences, M
card_text: Brief to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Prepared by: Dr. Ryan Van Lieshout, MD, PhD, FRCPC ([CONTACT]) Associate Professor, Department of Psychiatry and Behavioural Neurosciences, McMaster University Hamilton, ON The Perinatal Mental Health of Women, Pregnant Persons, Mothers and Birthing Parents in Canada: Perinatal mental health problems affect up to 1 in 5 mothers and birthing parents,1 rates that increased to 1 in 3 during the COVID-19 pandemic.2 Every case of postpartum depression (PPD) alone is associated with costs of up to $125,000 over the lifespan3 or $2.5B for each single year of births in Canada. The offspring of mothers with PPD are up to five times more likely to develop a clinically significant behavioural problem, and up to four times more likely to develop depression in their lives.4,5 Even though effective treatments can help both mothers and their children, as few as 1 in 10 pregnant and postpartum persons are able to access evidence-based care in Canada.6 Barriers to Care: There are many barriers to the receipt of timely perinatal mental health care in this country. One of the most significant is the current absence of structured and coordinated care pathways7 that identify sufferers and match the right person to the right treatment at the right time. Since most individuals with perinatal mental health problems will respond to psychotherapy and/or medications, another significant challenge is the limited knowledge possessed by front-line physicians about the relative safety of these medications during pregnancy and lactation. Name: BRIEF TO HOUSE OF COMMONS STANDING COMMITTEE ON HEALTH ON WOMEN’S HEALTH: MENTAL HEALTH By: Jeanne Sarson and Linda MacDonald,
card_text: BRIEF TO HOUSE OF COMMONS STANDING COMMITTEE ON HEALTH ON WOMEN’S HEALTH: MENTAL HEALTH By: Jeanne Sarson and Linda MacDonald, [ORG] March 31, 2024 Victim-Centered Knowledge The victim-centered relational knowledge shared is specific to the population of women who as children and or as adults survived torture committed by non-State actors. Our Grassroots Science began in 1993, in Nova Scotia. Laws, policies, and practices have nationally imposed discriminatory silencing on women’s mental health and their ability to openly address the impacts of non-State torture victimization in a manner that supports their human dignity, hope in their healability, and in being healed. Definition of Non-State Torture (NST) It is torture committed by non-State actors in public or in private, in relationships, perpetrated within families, in human trafficking, in prostitution, in pornographic exploitation, by violent groups and gangs, dismissed as socio-cultural, traditional, or religious acts or norms, and can be committed during migration, displacement, in humanitarian and civil unrest. It involves intentional infliction of, for example, mental or physical severe pain and suffering with electric shocking, water torture, forced drugging, group raping, beatings, whippings, cut, burnt, impregnations and abortions.1 Understanding Non-State Torturers’ Victimizations The following chart illustrates how forms of physical torture can progress into sexualized torture, and inflict psychological consequences perpetrated by non-State torturers. Explanations of these victimizations that damage women’s mental wellness include: Relational terrorism. Women developed hypervigilance in their efforts to survive chronic victimizations. In a survey from the Menopause Foundation of Canada (MFC) in 2022 in Canada, one in two women felt unprepared for menopause and 4 in 10 women felt alone during this 7me. 3 The effect of symptoms on work productivity is being recognized worldwide, including in Canada, with a reported $3.5 billion per year proposed loss to the economy from lost productivity, missed days of work and lost income. 4 Up to 10% of women will quit their jobs because of severe symptoms that impact their ability to function. With midlife women at the prime of their work life, these numbers are very concerning. Furthermore, untreated moderate to severe vasomotor symptoms has been linked to negative outcomes later in life including cardiovascular disease. 5 Inadequate Care for Menopause in Canada With more than 10 million women in Canada currently at the age of perimenopause or post menopause the 7me is now to ensure there is adequate support for midlife women. Name: Submission to the Standing Committee on Health Study on Women’s Health – Women’s Cancers Date: February 12, 2024 Submitted by: Dr Shannon Salvador
org: The Society of Gynecologic Oncology of Canada
card_text: Submission to the Standing Committee on Health Study on Women’s Health – Women’s Cancers Date: February 12, 2024 Submitted by: Dr Shannon Salvador President-Elect, Society of Gynecologic Oncology of Canada (GOC) Associate Professor, McGill University Head of Colposcopy, Jewish General Hospital Program Director, McGill Gynecologic Oncology Residency Program A history of gynecologic oncology and the Society of Gynecologic Oncologists of Canada (GOC) Historically, women’s cancers have been orphaned from traditional cancer care models. In the 1970s and 80’s a dedicated group of physicians created the gynecologic oncology sub-specialty to care for women with cancers of the ovary, uterus, cervix, vulva, and placenta. Among all surgical cancer specialties, ours is unique because the care is comprehensive, including diagnosis, surgery, systemic treatments (e.g., chemotherapy), surveillance, and palliative care. Only recently has patient care incorporated other disciplines, such as dedicated medical oncologists, radiation oncologists, palliative care teams and specialized nurses have begun collaborating with gynecologic oncology teams. Due to such historic isolation and separation from the traditional oncology care model, these cancers have long been deemed a “women’s issue.” Funding for clinical care and research has not kept pace with other, more common, cancers, such as colorectal, breast and lung. GOC, a non-profit organization, was created 40 years ago as a forum for medical professionals to highlight issues in women’s cancer care across Canada and help correct the disparity in cancer care access and equity in research funding for new treatments1. The vision of GOC is to be the voice of gynecologic cancer

Name: Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Study on Women’s Health Transforming Canada’s Gynecologic Cancer Care: Evidence-Based, Equitable Access for All

card_text: Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Study on Women’s Health Transforming Canada’s Gynecologic Cancer Care: Evidence-Based, Equitable Access for All February 6, 2024 Submitted by: Gillian Hanley and Jessica McAlpine Ariadne Holness de Hiller David Huntsman Nancy McKinstry CM Gavin Stuart Michelle Woo Please address any correspondence to: [CONTACT] and [CONTACT] Over half of Canada’s population is at risk of developing a gynecological cancer over their lifetime. Gynecologic cancer is any cancer that starts in the reproductive organs. The five types of gynecologic cancer are: cervical, ovarian, uterine, vaginal and vulvar. Gynecological cancer has significant socioeconomic impact, both in direct costs [e.g, in 2021, $781M direct health system costs] and incalculable indirect costs related to loss of productivity and societal impacts of the disease.(1) Clearly, improved prevention, diagnosis and treatment, and survivorship, are key to mitigating the burden and impact of gynecological cancers. In Canada, there are profound disparities in cancer care delivery within and between provinces.(1) Differences between provincial healthcare policies means that a woman affected by gynecologic cancer may receive dramatically different care in Alberta, Ontario, or Newfoundland. The disparities are greater in areas where access to, or receipt of, cancer care is compromised due to geographic, cultural, political or socio- economic factors. Equitable, culturally-appropriate, trauma-informed delivery of care remains a significant challenge. In 2019, in British Columbia, we created the Gynecologic Cancer Initiative. We are committed to improving access to menopause care for Canadian women. Menopause is the permanent cessation of ovarian function, and this clinically presents as no menstruation for twelve months. Menopause is a turning point in a woman’s life where her risks of osteoporosis, uterine prolapse, recurrent bladder infections, cardiovascular disease, and dementia increase significantly. Even though menopause is a universal stage of life for all women, there are few opportunities for training and physicians often feel inadequately prepared to offer menopause care even as more female patients are demanding it. Providing a comprehensive assessment at menopause is paramount to healthy aging for women and a critical juncture to prevent chronic disease. Name: WOMEN’S HEALTH STUDY House of Commons Standing Committee on Health The Impact of the Canadian Breast Screening Guidelines on my Diagnosis
card_text: WOMEN’S HEALTH STUDY House of Commons Standing Committee on Health The Impact of the Canadian Breast Screening Guidelines on my Diagnosis Annie Slight, B.A. Psychoeducation I had my first mammogram at age 40. I didn’t have a family history of breast cancer, I was young and healthy, so when the results came back normal, I was relieved, and just moved on. A few months later, I felt a small lump in my right breast. I didn’t worry too much-I had always had lumpy breasts-but still scheduled an appointment with my gynecologist. Following a physical exam, he said it was one of my normal glands, noting that my recent mammogram had come back normal so I shouldn’t worry. I trusted him and trusted my mammogram. The mass was growing, so I went back to see my doctor, who again reassured me, but finally sent me for an ultrasound. I remember that day like it was yesterday. The radiologist asked me to stay for a biopsy. I had one done that day and a few days later, I was diagnosed with stage 2, grade 3 breast cancer, over a year after feeling the small lump. I was 42. It’s only after my diagnosis that I started hearing about my breast density; I was informed that I had dense breasts, which increased my risk of developing breast cancer. I was told that looking for cancer on my mammogram was like looking for a polar bear in a snowstorm, as dense tissue appears white

The [ORG] advocates on issues that impact the musculoskeletal health of Canadians. Chiropractors are one of Canada’s experts in the assessment, diagnosis, and treatment of musculoskeletal (MSK) conditions, such as back pain, headaches, arm or neck strain and diseases of the muscle and joints, as well as the management of pain caused by these conditions. Each year, more than 11 million Canadians suffer from musculoskeletal conditions and by 2031 this number is projected to grow to an alarming 15 million.1 MSK conditions are having a devastating impact on the health, quality of life, and workforce participation of Canadians, as well as on Canada’s economy. Women’s Health: MSK Conditions and Chronic Pain According to the World Health Organization, MSK conditions are the leading cause of disability around the globe.2 These chronic conditions have a considerable impact on the quality of life of individuals, families, communities, and healthcare systems. Women consistently demonstrate more prevalent and severe clinical presentations of MSK conditions.3 Chronic pain is more common among Canadian women compared to men across all ages, with women aged 65 years and older consistently reporting the highest prevalence of chronic pain.4 In 2019, an estimated 7.63 million, or one in four Canadians aged 15 or older, were living with chronic pain. card_text: Women’s Health Study Shira Farber Breast Cancer Patient/ Screening Advocate Living with Advanced Breast Cancer: The Impact of Being Denied a Mammogram in my Forties Submission To: The House of Commons Standing Committee on Health My name is Shira Farber and I am a resident of Ontario. I am the mother of three, a business owner, a community volunteer and a proud Canadian citizen. I have spoken on the issue of the need for better national screening guidelines publicly and in the media, advocating both provincially and federally. I was invited to Canadian Parliament to meet with former Health Minister Yves Duclos and the Women’s Caucus with the support of my MP, the Honorable Ya’ara Saks and have continued to actively engage with members of Parliament and Public Health about this important issue. When I found an abnormality in my breast, almost two years ago, just before my 48th birthday, it was already considered an advanced stage cancer in the form of three solid large tumors and was suspected to have been growing for some time. When I had asked my family doctor on two separate occasions about screening after I turned 40, I was advised that Canadian guidelines recommended against mammograms before the age of fifty, due to too many “false positives”. I had great respect for my family doctor and always had faith that her guidance was based on current evidence. Name: Submission to The House of Commons Standing Committee on Health
card_text: Submission to The House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Women’s Health Study Breast Cancer Screening Recommendations in Canada - A Patient’s Experience Rosilene Kraft, MBA, M.Sc., Ph.D., P.Eng., living with de novo stage IV breast cancer since 2018 Dear Members of the Standing Committee on Health: I am writing to you with the intent of providing personal evidence of the risks of not providing recommendations for women to get regular screening mammograms between the ages of 40 and 50 years old and how the current guidelines have impacted my cancer diagnosis. I live in Coquitlam, British Columbia. I am a professional engineer with Master and PhD degrees in engineering and an MBA in Industrial Administration. I am married and have a bright 9-year-old daughter with autism. I was diagnosed with stage IV breast cancer in 2018 at the age of 47. It was already metastatic from the initial diagnosis, and, with that, I could never receive treatment with curative intent. Because of my heterogeneously dense breast tissue and because I had found masses during self-exams, I had started having yearly mammograms since the age of 35, when I lived in Brazil. This schedule remained the same when, at 38, I moved to the United States. I then married a Canadian citizen and moved to Canada in 2011.
