Name: HOUSE OF COMMONS STANDING COMMITTEE ON HEALTH Women’s Health Study SUBMITTED BY: Dr. Kathleen Ross, President
card_text: HOUSE OF COMMONS STANDING COMMITTEE ON HEALTH Women’s Health Study SUBMITTED BY: Dr. Kathleen Ross, President Canadian Medical Association Introduction Since 1867, the Canadian Medical Association (CMA) has advocated for physicians and championed the health of Canadians. The CMA brings a unique perspective on women’s health, with a focus on the women physicians who care for Canadians. Their health holds significance beyond the way it affects their own well‐being. As Canada’s health care is being provided increasingly by women physicians, the wellness of this demographic is becoming of greater and greater importance to the larger public. It is getting harder to ignore a central truth — the health of our women physicians is suffering. Women physicians There are myriad systemic issues that continue to negatively influence the ability of women physicians to flourish under the current conditions of their work life. More and more commonly, women physicians are experiencing worsening wellness outcomes, with significantly higher rates of burnout and screening positive for depression. According to the CMA’s National Physician Health Survey results (2021), burnout among women physicians is a shocking 16% higher than among their male counterparts.1 The pre‐ pandemic prevalence of suicidal ideation was significantly higher among women in medicine than among men and has remained high since 2021. Workload accounts for some of the imbalance in burnout. Administrative tasks make up a larger portion of women physicians’ weeks. Women physicians are over 10% more likely than men to be caregivers for children, parents or other family members. The

Name: Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Women’s Health Study December 15, 2023 Submitted by:
card_text: Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Women’s Health Study December 15, 2023 Submitted by: Janet Ko, President and Co-Founder The [ORG] Perimenopause is the time leading up to menopause and can last anywhere from six to eight years. During perimenopause estrogen and progesterone levels fluctuate – sometimes dramatically – leading to many symptoms that can have a negative impact on health and quality of life. Most women are in perimenopause between the ages of 40 and 50. Women are postmenopausal for the rest of their lives. Women experience symptoms for years, sometimes decades, and yet, they are not supported by the health system, their employers, or society as a whole. Menopause: a women’s health issue There are over 30 symptoms associated with perimenopause and menopause, including but not limited to lack of energy, depression, flushing and night sweats, muscle and joint ache, insomnia, bladder control issues, heart palpitations and headaches. There are too many to be included in a brief. Canadian women typically have a low awareness of the symptoms associated with menopause/perimenopauseiv. Every day millions of women in Canada are suffering through menopause without understanding what is happening to their bodies and where to turn for help. Beyond the symptoms, once a woman has reached menopause she is at greater risk for osteoporosis, heart disease and genitourinary issues. org: Action Canada for Sexual Health and Rights
card_text: 1 Submission to the Standing Committee on Health Study on Women’s Health November 30th, 2023 1 [ORG] works to promote health, well-being, and rights related to sexuality and reproduction. We provide direct support, referrals, and information to the public and work with decision-makers to advance progressive policies on access to abortion, stigma-free healthcare, gender equality, 2SLGBTQ+ rights, and inclusive sex-ed. Our vision is for all people everywhere to have full control over, and to decide freely upon, all matters related to their sexuality, reproduction, and gender, including their reproductive and sexual health. For more information, contact Insiya Mankani, Public Affairs Office ([CONTACT]) or Kelly Bowden, Director of Policy and Advocacy ([CONTACT]) Introduction Health as defined by the World Health Organization is not only the absence of disease or infirmity, but the complete physical, mental, and social well-being of each person. The WHO states that “the enjoyment of the highest attainable standard of health is one of the fundamental rights of every human being without distinction of race, religion, political belief, economic or social condition”. In short – a woman’s right to health is equal to all others, and her health is defined by her whole of being, not strictly the management of diseases. Despite this, significant gaps remain for women across our country to fulfill their right to health. Specifically addressing the unmet needs of women’s sexual and reproductive health is both crucial for addressing the individual needs of women and to addressing wider social goals such as gender equality.

Name: Submission to The House of Commons’ Standing Committee on Health Study on Women’s Health – Endometriosis and Gynecological conditions
card_text: Submission to The House of Commons’ Standing Committee on Health Study on Women’s Health – Endometriosis and Gynecological conditions Date: November 29th 2023 Submission by: Dr. Andrew Zakhari Dr. Dong Bach Nguyen Representing: MUHC EndoCARES [ORG] – Endometriosis Centre for the Advancement of Research and Surgery What is already known Endometriosis affects one in ten women and untold numbers of gender diverse people. It can cause debilitating pain, infertility, and significant emotional and psychological distress. This condition is characterized by an abnormal growth of highly inflammatory tissue within the abdomen or pelvis (similar to the inner lining of the uterus) that causes scarring, and can invade into other organs like the bowel or bladder. Less commonly, it may even affect pelvic nerves (like the sciatic nerve) or more remote areas such as the lungs or diaphragm. Endometriosis can be severe enough to cause loss of kidney function, to cause blockage of intestines, to produce large, inflammatory cysts in the pelvis, and much more. We know there is a lengthy time to first seeing a specialist, let alone obtaining a diagnosis, which may take up to a decade.1 Endometriosis is largely considered a benign condition, but we also know there is an increased risk of ovarian and peritoneal cancers in these patients, although this is poorly understood. Absenteeism from work or school and poor quality of life are sadly quite commonplace for patients with endometriosis, and there is a huge economic burden associated with it.2-4 Lastly, treating endometriosis correctly is challenging

card_text: Submission to the Standing Committee on Health Women’s Health Study November 2023 The [ORG] ([ORG]) welcomes the opportunity to submit to the Standing Committee on Health’s study of Women’s Health. We would like to acknowledge that the Standing Committee on Health is comprised of 11 male members out of a total of 12. Despite efforts to enhance female representation through seat exchanges among members, it is disconcerting that the committee does not align with the gender parity goals of our government. We strongly encourage Members of Parliament across all political affiliations to prioritize gender parity in the selection of committee members, thus preventing any backslide in our collective efforts. Summary of Recommendations: 1. Address the long-standing health inequities faced by marginalized and vulnerable women. 2. Collaborate with provincial and territorial governments in creating a strategy to improve senior women’s health in Canada. 3. Establish protected policies and provisions that will not only support victims of gender-based violence but also prevent those from becoming victims in the first place. 4. Collaborate with provinces and territories in ensuring equitable access to abortion care. 5. Protect the interest and rights of LGBTQ+ Canadians by working with provincial and territorial governments to improve access to gender affirming care. Marginalized and Vulnerable Women The [ORG] calls on the federal government to address the long-standing health inequities faced by marginalized and vulnerable women. Name: Addressing Gendered Islamophobia in Canadian Healthcare for Women Submission to the Standing Committee on Health for the Women’s Health Study
card_text: Addressing Gendered Islamophobia in Canadian Healthcare for Women Submission to the Standing Committee on Health for the Women’s Health Study November 17, 2023 dignity, stereotypical comments, prejudiced attitudes, and aggressive behaviour are only some of the experiences of Muslim patients at different healthcare settings. Studies describe instances where discrimination may have led to patients receiving suboptimal care and services from healthcare providers. Participants felt that discrimination led to them getting late diagnoses, being considered for discharge early, and not being taken seriously when describing symptoms and pain levels. Muslim patients also felt they were not offered services due to the personal prejudices of their healthcare providers. The scoping review also demonstrates that Muslims receive culturally inappropriate care due to health care staff’s lack of awareness of Islamic practices, such as the need for prayer spaces, modesty and privacy for patients, halal food options, and hygienic services. While this is not always considered a form of discrimination, patients describe having negative healthcare experiences because of it.2 One study from Newfoundland that looked at maternity health needs of immigrant Muslim women found that women experienced insensitivity and lack of knowledge about their religious and cultural practices. The study found that health information was limited or lacked the cultural and religious specificity to meet their needs during pregnancy, labour and delivery, and postpartum phases.3 Similarly, a study conducted by the Canadian Mental Health Association found that there is a lack of cultural understanding on the part of many service providers, and there is

Name: ISSUE
card_text: ISSUE Contraception is not easily accessible through the province of Alberta. Financial and gatekeeping barriers must be resolved in order to provide comprehensive reproductive care for Albertans. PURPOSE To equitably provide free contraception to all Canadians for their medical needs and reproductive rights. RECOMMENDATION Health Canada address the gap in contraception access in Alberta by funding all forms of contraception including hormonal contraceptives, intrauterine devices (IUDs), condoms and emergency contraception pills. Pharmacies, schools, and government agencies should distribute these medications/products at no cost to improve access–especially to vulnerable populations. Health Canada should also fund additional resources for nurses in schools, prisons, shelters and non-governmental organizations (NGOs). RATIONALE The Canada Health Act requires publicly funded medically necessary services be provided, yet many in Canada either cannot access or cannot afford appropriate contraception (Chaudhary, 2021). Access is not equitable in every province, with British Columbia as the only province providing universal provision of contraception for their whole population (Global Contraception Policy Atlas, 2023). As with all good public health programs, expanding support for sexual and reproductive health saves lives and resources; comparable programs actually save government spending in a variety of areas as well as support the overall health of the population. The cost savings are estimated as: o The cost of delivering universal contraception across Canada would be $157 million. Name: Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Study on Women’s Health Screening for perinatal depression in
card_text: Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Study on Women’s Health Screening for perinatal depression in Canada A brief submitted on behalf of the [ORG] by: Catriona Hippman, PhD CGC, Postdoctoral Fellow, University of Calgary, BC Reproductive Mental Health Program Nicole Letourneau, PhD RN FCAHS FAAN FCAN FRSC, University of Calgary Research Excellence Chair in Parent and Child Health, Professor, Faculty of Nursing Lisa Gagnon, MD FRCPC, Psychiatrist, Perinatal Mental Health Network Chair, Alberta Health Services Prescilla Carrion, MSc, CGC, CCGC, Research Manager & Clinical Associate Professor, [ORG] Deirdre Ryan, MB FRCPC, Psychiatrist, Medical Director, BC Reproductive Mental Health Program Oct. 3, 2023 http://www.bcwomens.ca/our-services/specialized-services/reproductive-mentalhealth#Our--Services Phone: [CONTACT] Contacts: Dr. Deirdre Ryan, Dr. Catriona Hippman, Medical Director; Postdoctoral Fellow [CONTACT] [CONTACT] Background Perinatal depression is common; affecting 10-15% of women1–4. Rates are even higher amongst marginalized groups, affecting almost half (50%) of immigrant women5 and Indigenous women6. In light of this, it is unsurprising that suicide is a leading cause of maternal deaths in Canada7–9. Further, the costs of untreated perinatal depression and anxiety in the UK have been estimated at 6.6 billion GBP for births in the year 2013 alone10. In a 2021 report by the Canadian Perinatal Mental Health Collaborative, extrapolating these data to the Canadian population demonstrated a cost of 150,000 CAD for each mother-baby dyad affected by perinatal depression and anxiety11. However, perinatal depression can be successfully treated, particularly with early identification, and negative downstream consequences for the child and family can thus be prevented12

Name: The Role of Physiotherapy Expertise in Women’s Health A Submission to the Federal Standing Committee on Health for the Women’s Health Study (October 5, 2023)
card_text: The Role of Physiotherapy Expertise in Women’s Health A Submission to the Federal Standing Committee on Health for the Women’s Health Study (October 5, 2023) The Role of Physiotherapy Expertise in Supporting Women’s Health & Well-Being: Ensuring that women receive access to physiotherapy care in Canada is essential as it provides numerous health benefits including improved health outcomes, reduced healthcare costs, increased equality, empowerment, and self-autonomy. Physiotherapy professionals are highly skilled at addressing specific women’s health issues such as pelvic floor dysfunction, pregnancy-related musculoskeletal conditions, postpartum recovery, menstrual pain management, pre-and post-surgical rehabilitation, management of chronic pain conditions, osteoporosis management, breast health, and lymphedema. Recommendation 1: Ensure that women in Canada have equitable, publicly funded access to pelvic health physiotherapy. Physiotherapists play a crucial role in supporting women with a variety of needs, but access to pelvic health physiotherapy is limited despite being established as a first-line treatment for a variety of issues1. Pelvic health physiotherapy is well-established in many countries globally but continues to be an area of growth for the Canadian healthcare system. France, for example, has prioritized this care with a publicly funded pelvic floor rehabilitation program that provides post-natal patients with 10, and up to 20 pelvic health physiotherapy sessions. This unique program allows women to re-educate their pelvic floor following pregnancy and to resolve common issues such as urinary or fecal incontinence, pelvic organ prolapse, and pelvic pain conditions2 3. Name: HOUSE OF COMMONS STANDING COMMITTEE ON HEALTH Women’s Health Study SUBMITTED BY: Linda Silas, President
card_text: HOUSE OF COMMONS STANDING COMMITTEE ON HEALTH Women’s Health Study SUBMITTED BY: Linda Silas, President [ORG] Introduction The [ORG] ([ORG]) is the largest nurses’ organization in Canada, representing 250,000 frontline unionized nurses and nursing students across the country. Our members work in every sector of health care – from home care and long-term care to community and acute care – and practice as licensed/registered practical nurses, registered nurses, registered psychiatric nurses and nurse practitioners. Nurses are the largest regulated profession in health care with a total of 466,014 regulated nurses eligible to practice in Canada in 2022, as identified by the Canadian Institute for Health Information, and an estimated 90% of them are women.1 There are many workplace issues in health care that have significant health consequences for nurses, regardless of the individual nurse’s sex or gender. However, since a vastly disproportionate number of nurses are women, it is important to recognize these health issues as directly impacting women’s health in Canada. Amid a health human resources crisis, with record nurse vacancies in health workplaces across the country, it is even more imperative that the issues contained in this brief are addressed with robust support from the federal government. Mental health Before the pandemic, one in three nurses screened positive for major depressive disorder, one in four screened positive for generalized anxiety disorder and clinical burnout, one in three reported having suicidal thoughts, and over 20% screened positive for post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) and panic disorder. Name: WOMEN’S HEALTH DATE: May 1, 2023 To:
card_text: WOMEN’S HEALTH DATE: May 1, 2023 To: Standing Committee on Health From: [ORG] Canada RE: BRIEF PRESENTED TO THE STANDING COMMITTEE ON HEALTH [ORG]: Here for her health [ORG] Canada1 is a life sciences company that works to improve women’s health every day. Working in areas such as contraception, fertility and menopause, as well as inflammatory diseases, respiratory health and women’s cancers, the organization is constantly exploring new opportunities and innovative treatment options to address the unmet medical needs of patients in Canada, including endometriosis, polycystic ovary syndrome and postpartum hemorrhage. In addition, through its growing portfolio of biosimilars, including the recent launch of two oncology biosimilars, [ORG] aims to accelerate the adoption of biosimilars in Canada, which will generate savings for health care systems that can be reinvested to improve access to innovative therapies for Canadians. [ORG] Canada works closely with health professionals, patient groups and decision makers to ensure that its health care solutions are accessible to the greatest number of patients. Equity and parity issues are also at the heart of the company’s concerns. The company has implemented sustainable business practices to reduce its impact on the environment and is actively involved in its community, for example with Le Chaînon in Montreal. Name: Submission to the Standing Committee on Health Women’s Health Study September 9, 2022 Submitted by:
card_text: Submission to the Standing Committee on Health Women’s Health Study September 9, 2022 Submitted by: Benjamin Davis Senior Vice-President, Mission The Multiple Sclerosis (MS) Society of Canada Introduction The Multiple Sclerosis (MS) Society of Canada is pleased to provide this submission to the Standing Committee on Health’s study on women’s health. Canada has one of the highest rates of multiple sclerosis (MS) in the world. An estimated 90,000 Canadians live with the autoimmune disease and, on average, 12 Canadians are diagnosed every day. Women are three times more likely to be diagnosed with MS than men. Most people are diagnosed with MS between the ages of 20 and 49 – the age at which many women are starting their careers and perhaps building a family – and the unpredictable effects of the disease will last for the rest of their lives. MS is classified as an autoimmune disease of the central nervous system. The immune system attacks myelin, the protective covering of the nerves in the brain, which disrupts communication between the central nervous system and the rest of the body. The disease course of MS is unpredictable, often occurring in a pattern of relapses and remissions, also known as an episodic disability. There is currently no cure for MS, though multiple disease-modifying therapies (DMTs) exist to help manage symptoms. The immense socioeconomic impact of MS for women The cost of paying for medication, services, equipment, and treatment is a significant economic burden for people with MS and their families.

card_text: Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health Written Testimony by Leanne McConnachie [ORG] Canada Thank you, Madame Chair, and House Committee members, for the opportunity to submit the following brief. We fully support the use of antimicrobials for metaphylactic or therapeutic treatment, but not for prophylactic purposes. card_text: May 5, 2026 House of Commons Standing Committee on Health (HESA) Study on Canada’s Pharmaceutical Sovereignty Executive Summary The [ORG] ([ORG]), now known as Vital - Canada’s Pharmaceutical Supply Chain Alliance, represents the companies responsible for delivering the vast majority of medicines across Canada. From this system-wide vantage point, pharmaceutical distributors have a unique understanding of how medicines move from global supply to patient care, and how policy decisions impact access. Pharmaceutical sovereignty is an important policy objective, often framed in terms of domestic manufacturing and reducing reliance on international suppliers. While these are important considerations, ensuring that medicines reach patients reliably requires a broader system perspective. Ultimately, pharmaceutical sovereignty is determined by whether medicines reach patients reliably, safely, and on time. Canada already has a critical national asset that supports this objective: a highly regulated, nationally integrated pharmaceutical distribution system that ensures physical access to medicines across the country every day. Distribution is the system that connects supply to patients. Distributors operate at the centre of the supply chain, connecting hundreds of manufacturers to over 12,000 points of dispensing. From this position, they see how policy decisions—on pricing, procurement, and manufacturing—directly affect access to medicines for Canadians. Canada will continue to rely on global supply chains for a significant share of its medicines. The policy objective should therefore be to ensure that this supply can be reliably accessed and delivered to patients. Vital recommends that the Committee: 1. Recognize pharmaceutical distribution as critical health system infrastructure and ensure that

Name: Canada’s Pharmaceutical Sovereignty
card_text: Canada’s Pharmaceutical Sovereignty National pharmaceutical sovereignty ensures that Canadians have reliable access to highquality medicines and treatments when they need them. This means having a strong domestic manufacturing capacity and integration into key supply chains. Medicines and the systems that produce them sit at the intersection of public health, economic growth and national security. When a country’s pharmaceutical industry is dependent on foreign-owned firms and imported inputs, it may not reflect domestic priorities, leaving the country vulnerable to market forces and supply chain disruptions. Canada relies heavily on global supply chains for access to pharmaceuticals: almost all active pharmaceutical ingredients used in Canada are imported, with a majority from China and India, creating a concentration risk in critical inputs.i Canada also faces persistent and significant drug shortages. A stable supply is necessary to keep costs down, avoid expensive solutions to sudden shocks or emergencies and maintain access for all Canadians. The government should seek to bolster Canada’s biopharma capacity and self-suIiciency through targeted policy action and closer collaboration with industry. Other countries already recognize domestic drug manufacturing as a core component of national security. The United States’ Defence Production Act makes explicit that America views building biopharmaceutical resilience as an important national security priority. Similarly, the European Union’s Critical Medicines Act seeks to modernize and expand its domestic pharmaceutical manufacturing capacity. Canada must act to support its own pharmaceutical and biomanufacturing sectors to ensure resiliency. Name: Submission Brief Canada’s Pharmaceutical Sovereignty May 5, 2026 [ORG]
card_text: Submission Brief Canada’s Pharmaceutical Sovereignty May 5, 2026 [ORG] Submitted to Standing Committee on Health (HESA) House of Commons Executive Summary The goal of strengthening Canada’s pharmaceutical sovereignty is widely shared, and strengthening domestic supply and improving patient access is critical to realize this goal. But successful pharmaceutical sovereignty requires a balanced, whole-system approach that includes all aspects of the innovation and pharmaceutical supply sectors. A narrow policy lens that favours domestic biomanufacturing in isolation must carefully consider downstream implications specifically on Canadians; those for whom these policies are being considered. Biomanufacturing is deeply connected with global supply chains, drawing on a complex, globally distributed network of innovators and suppliers, each providing critical components. Any policy that solely prioritizes domestic biomanufacturing, without an equal investment in other aspects of the pharmaceutical supply chain and innovation, can reduce competitive pressure, slow development, constrain supply and ultimately exacerbate the current risk of increased drug shortages as well as increasing costs and barriers to patient access. Pharmaceutical sovereignty is often discussed in terms of manufacturing capacity and upstream supply, but it must ultimately be measured by whether medicines reliably reach patients. True sovereignty is realized at the point of care. While strengthening domestic production and diversifying supply sources are important objectives, they are insufficient on their own if medicines cannot be accessed, substituted, or sustained when the system experiences disruption. Community pharmacies represent the last mile of Canada’s pharmaceutical system and are where fragility can impact health care. Every day, pharmacies manage shortages

Name: Written Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health (HESA) Study on Canada’s Pharmaceutical Sovereignty Submitted by: Andrew Booth, CFO
card_text: Written Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health (HESA) Study on Canada’s Pharmaceutical Sovereignty Submitted by: Andrew Booth, CFO May 5, 2026 Executive Summary Canada has the scientific foundation to be a global leader in biotechnology, yet it continues to capture only a fraction of the economic and strategic value generated by its own innovation. While Canadian researchers and companies are highly successful in discovering and advancing promising new therapies, these assets are too often sold to foreign firms prior to commercialization. This results in the loss of intellectual property (IP), high-value jobs, assets for national security, and long-term economic returns that could otherwise benefit Canadians. Pharmaceutical sovereignty cannot be achieved solely through supply chain measures or by relying on multinational firms to operate within Canada. Instead, it requires a deliberate strategy to build globally competitive, Canadian-headquartered biopharmaceutical companies that retain IP, scale domestically, and commercialize medicines from within Canada. Achieving this objective will require targeted policy reforms, including modernizing the SR&ED program to support growth-stage firms, implementing a Patent Box regime to incentivize IP retention, investing in critical infrastructure, and defining what constitutes a “Canadian Company” that is poised to scale up in a modern, globally integrated industry. Our Recommendations Contained herein are AbCellera’s recommendations on securing Canada’s pharmaceutical sovereignty. We are recommending the following: 1. Create a definition of what is a “Canadian Company” to align with our national interest in scaling companies domestically. Eligibility should focus on the location of headquarters, senior leadership, IP

card_text: Brief on the consultation regarding Canada’s pharmaceutical sovereignty by [ORG] ([ORG]) to the Standing Committee on Health (HESA) May 5, 2026 Introduction The [ORG] is a [ORG]-based coalition of labour organizations, community groups, citizens’ committees and organizations representing feminists, seniors, people with disabilities and caregivers. Our work is grounded in defending the core principles that have shaped the health system since its inception: public ownership, free access, availability, universality and comprehensiveness. For many years, we have advocated for the implementation of a universal public pharmacare plan. On several occasions, we have emphasized that any future plan must be publicly administered, universal, accessible, equitable, viable, independent and respectful of provincial jurisdiction.1 In our view, the issue of Canada’s pharmaceutical sovereignty has become a matter of fundamental importance in the current context. The COVID-19 pandemic exposed Canada’s dependence on foreign companies not only for medicines but also for a broad range of medical supplies. The challenges surrounding vaccine procurement during the crisis also underscored the importance of being able to rely on domestic production capacity. Such capacity becomes especially critical when other countries attempt to restrict or reduce exports of pharmaceutical products or seek to limit the use of licences that allow third parties to manufacture patented products. The U.S.-led trade war is creating significant uncertainty about the possibility of tariffs on pharmaceuticals. Name: May 5, 2026 Submission to the Standing Committee on Health’s Study on Canada’s Pharmaceutical Sovereignty Executive Summary
card_text: May 5, 2026 Submission to the Standing Committee on Health’s Study on Canada’s Pharmaceutical Sovereignty Executive Summary The [ORG] ([ORG]) welcomes the opportunity to contribute to the Standing Committee on Health’s (HESA) study on Canada’s pharmaceutical sovereignty. Canada’s ability to ensure reliable, timely, and equitable access to medicines is a foundational element of both public health and economic resilience. From a patient perspective, pharmaceutical sovereignty must be understood not as isolation or domestic self-sufficiency, but as strategic resilience. This means the committee must consider how to protect access to medically necessary drugs across therapeutic areas, ensure continuity of supply, attract innovation and early launches, and deliver equitable access for all patients in Canada. This includes medicines for common chronic conditions, cancer and rare cancers, and therapies for rare diseases, which can be sensitive to market uncertainty, regulatory delays, and pricing instability. Pharmaceutical sovereignty is particularly consequential for Canadians living with cancer, rare cancers, and rare diseases, where treatment timelines are often narrow and therapies increasingly personalized. Delays in regulatory approval, clinical trial start-up, or reimbursement decisions can result in missed treatment windows, poorer health outcomes, and premature death. For patients with rare diseases, including rare cancers, where standard-of-care options may be limited or non-existent, access to innovative medicines and trials are often the only viable therapeutic pathway. A sovereignty framework that does not explicitly include a strategy to improve the development of and early access to innovative therapies – including innovative oncology and rare disease therapies - risks further entrenching

Name: Rebuilding Canada’s Insulin Production Capacity to Ensure National Health Security Submission to Standing Committee on Health’s Study on Canada’s Pharmaceutical Sovereignty
card_text: Rebuilding Canada’s Insulin Production Capacity to Ensure National Health Security Submission to Standing Committee on Health’s Study on Canada’s Pharmaceutical Sovereignty By [ORG] Canada April 2026 Background & Context Discovered by Canadian scientists in 1921, insulin was produced domestically for 65 years at Toronto’s Connaught Laboratories, supplying Canadians affordably. However, after Connaught’s privatization in the 1980s, Canada ceased all insulin manufacturing by the mid-1990s and now imports 100% of its insulin1. Today, all insulin used by Canadians comes from a few large companies based outside Canada – notably Eli Lilly (U.S.), Novo Nordisk (Denmark), and Sanofi (France) – which produce insulin in their facilities abroad. This complete import reliance means Canada is fully dependent on foreign supply chains for a life-sustaining medication. Importantly, this paper does not recommend replacing or scaling down Canada’s existing insulin importation system. Canada’s current supply relies appropriately on trusted international manufacturers, and those relationships should continue. Rather, this submission recommends that Canada establish domestic manufacturing capability as a strategic contingency to be activated in emergencies, prolonged shortages, or major global disruptions so that Canadians are never left without access to insulin. Immediate Life-Saving Need Insulin is not just another pharmaceutical product; it is a critical, non-substitutable medicine. About 300,000 Canadians live with type 1 diabetes, meaning they cannot survive without daily insulin injections. For these individuals, any significant interruption in insulin access becomes life-threatening within days. Without insulin, a person with type 1 diabetes (and some with type 2 diabetes who are insulin dependent) can

card_text: WRITTEN SUBMISSION TO THE HOUSE OF COMMONS STANDING COMMITTEE ON HEALTH (HESA) Study: Canada’s Pharmaceutical Sovereignty Submitted by: [ORG] Date: April 22, 2026 Executive Summary Pharmaceutical sovereignty requires a balanced, coordinated approach that strengthens domestic capability while maintaining diversified, globally connected supply chains. As Canada’s national group purchasing organization serving over 2,100 hospitals and health-system providers, [ORG] has direct visibility into the vulnerabilities that lead to critical drug shortages, supply disruptions, and escalating costs. A sustainable strategy must prioritize: • ensuring uninterrupted patient access • building targeted domestic manufacturing capacity for clinically critical medicines • modernizing procurement to value reliability and redundancy • enhancing regulatory agility • strengthening supply-chain transparency through data sharing • aligning manufacturing, health, and innovation policy • combining strategic domestic capacity with diversified global sourcing Canada cannot achieve pharmaceutical sovereignty through isolation or full self-sufficiency. The most resilient model combines strategic domestic capability with diversified, allied global supply networks supported by coordinated national policy. Considerations Ensuring Reliable Access for Patients Uninterrupted access to safe, effective, and affordable medicines must remain the central objective of any pharmaceutical sovereignty strategy. Recent shortages, including sterile injectables, antimicrobials, and paediatric formulations have demonstrated how heavily Canada relies on globally concentrated supply chains for essential medicines. Hospitals and providers are increasingly forced to manage unpredictable supply, leading to increased risk with substitutions, rationing, and clinical workarounds that strain staff and can compromise patient care. Any policy changes must therefore be evaluated through the lens of reliable patient access and continuity of

card_text: Strengthening Canada’s Pharmaceutical Sovereignty Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health (HESA) From: [ORG] Inc. Date: April 2026 Executive summary Canada’s dependence on foreign pharmaceutical manufacturing, with 88% of generic medicines imported, exposes the country to rising global instability, fragile supply chains, and growing access gaps. Canada reports more active drug shortages than most OECD peers, while key supplier countries show high rates of regulatory non-compliance. The pandemic and ongoing geopolitical tensions have made clear that medicines are not ordinary goods but strategic assets essential to national security, economic stability, and public health. Pharmaceutical sovereignty means ensuring that Canadians have reliable, secure, and sustainable access to medicines through a strong domestic manufacturing and innovation base, supported by policies that keep Canadian production viable and competitive. True pharmaceutical sovereignty requires regulatory frameworks that accelerate access without disadvantaging Canadian producers, procurement tools that recognize domestic value-added, and targeted fiscal measures that make long-term investment in Canada attractive and sustainable. [ORG] recommends a coordinated policy package: 1. Recognize medicines as national security goods, enabling procurement and investment tools aligned with Canada’s sovereignty objectives. 2. Modernize regulation in a way that strengthens, not weakens, Canadian capacity, including a priority review pathway for Canadian developers and a narrow, high-value scope for the Reliance Order. 3. Reward domestic value-added through procurement criteria and fiscal measures, including a refundable profit-based- manufacturing tax credit, and federal investment incentives inspired by Québec’s Investment and Innovation Tax Credit (C3I), which supports modernization and advanced manufacturing upgrades (automation,

card_text: Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on HeDOWK H(6$ Study on: Canada's Pharmaceutical Sovereignty Submitted by: StpShanie 0ichaud, Ph' President and C(2, [ORG]  Canada's ImmunotheraSy 1etZorN $Sril  Executive Summary [ORG] – Canada's Immunotherapy Network – respectfully submits this brief to the Standing Committee on Health in support of its study on Canada's pharmaceutical sovereignty. Canada's discussion of pharmaceutical sovereignty has focused predominantly on supply-chain security: securing access to medicines manufactured elsewhere. That framing, while important, addresses only half the question. True pharmaceutical sovereignty also requires the capacity to create novel therapies on Canadian soil — to move Canadian discoveries from the laboratory to the clinic and into patients' hands. On that dimension, Canada's performance is measurably poor, and the cause is structural. In December 2025, [ORG] commissioned the Institute on Governance (IOG) to benchmark Canada's translational health research ecosystem against six international comparators. The finding is unambiguous: Canada does not lack excellent science or policy effort. It lacks the governance infrastructure to convert discovery into therapy. Advanced therapies — including the cell and gene therapies that represent the next generation of medicine — fall structurally between federal mandates: too applied for federal health research funding, too risky for private capital. The consequences are visible: only 3.4% of cancer immunotherapy clinical trials conducted in Canada between 2002 and March 2026 were based on made-in-Canada innovations. Canada is the only G7 nation without a domestic pharmaceutical company producing novel medicines. These are not market failures. They are governance

Name: A Brief to the Standing Committee on Health (HESA) March 27, 2026 Strengthening Canada’s Pharmaceutical Resilience Building Capacity Along the Value Chain in a Context of Strategic
card_text: A Brief to the Standing Committee on Health (HESA) March 27, 2026 Strengthening Canada’s Pharmaceutical Resilience Building Capacity Along the Value Chain in a Context of Strategic Interdependence Introduction Recent disruptions in supply chains have reignited discussions about the resilience of health care systems. The COVID-19 pandemic, geopolitical tensions and the concentration of certain industrial capacities have exposed how vulnerable many countries are to disruptions in the supply of drugs and health products. The conclusion is clear: Canada must take action to strengthen its position in this strategic sector. This means enhancing its research and development capacity, better capturing and retaining the value of domestically developed innovation and supporting robust industrial capacities. At the same time, it is essential to improve the conditions for accessing drugs to ensure that the health care system operates smoothly. In an environment marked by increasing geopolitical tension, a systemic approach is needed to strengthen pharmaceutical resilience in the long term. Part of the solution lies in developing local production capacities in several components of the pharmaceutical value chain. Several countries have taken steps in this direction to better secure their supply of health care products.1 However, the pharmaceutical industry is extremely globalized. The various stages of research, development and production are spread across multiple countries, and this international organization of value chains is now a structural characteristic of the sector. Pharmaceutical resilience does not rely solely on the self-sufficiency of certain products, but on a balance between developing local capacity and integrating into diversified

Name: Establishing a domestic supply chain for critical immunoglobulin therapy in Canada [ORG] operates the blood supply system in all provinces and territories except Quebec. Through an arm’s-length govern
card_text: Establishing a domestic supply chain for critical immunoglobulin therapy in Canada [ORG] operates the blood supply system in all provinces and territories except Quebec. Through an arm’s-length governance model, it is accountable to provincial and territorial ministers of health, who are its corporate members, and who provide operational funding. [ORG] is regulated by Health Canada. Within this framework, the organization is responsible and accountable to act in the best interests of the country by ensuring the safety and security of the supply of blood and blood products. As part of these responsibilities, the organization also manages a pan-Canadian formulary of about 50 plasma protein and related products, or PPRP, including immunoglobulin therapy made from human plasma. Human plasma carries immune-supporting proteins that specialized pharmaceutical companies manufacture into immunoglobulins through a process called fractionation. When someone's immune system fails, doctors turn to immunoglobulin therapy to provide antibodies to treat infections and immune-related conditions like primary immunodeficiencies, autoimmune diseases, and others. It is estimated that more than half (50 to 60 per cent) of patients in Canada prescribed immunoglobulins must take them to live, with no other treatment option available for their care. At a time when Canada’s governments and residents are focused on greater self-reliance — securing essential supply chains, building manufacturing capacity, and reaffirming the country’s sovereignty — [ORG] is implementing a comprehensive plan in keeping with these goals to ensure patients continue to have long-term access to immunoglobulins. The plan has multiple components and was informed by a robust

card_text: BRIEF FROM THE ASSOCIATION DES COLLÈGES ET UNIVERSITÉS DE LA FRANCOPHONIE CANADIENNE Study: Impact of Immigration Policy on Healthcare and Barriers to Integrating Internationally Trained Professionals Submitted to the Standing Committee on Health December 2025 [ORG] ▼ The [ORG] ([ORG]) is an association of 22 French-language and bilingual post-secondary institutions outside Quebec. The [ORG] represents the collective interests of its members and leads initiatives that bring about structural and lasting change. More specifically, it works to strengthen the continuum of French-language education by expanding access to post-secondary education in francophone minority communities (FMCs). The Consortium national de formation en santé (CNFS), a national health training consortium housed at the [ORG] and funded by Health Canada under the Official Languages Health Program, plays a key role in providing access to French-language health services in FMCs. The CNFS supports the recruitment and training of additional students in health programs, the integration of graduates into FMCs, and health-related research in FMCs. In short, the CNFS increases the number of health professionals able to deliver health services in French, thereby supporting the vitality of FMCs. Recently, the CNFS national secretariat commissioned a study on possible solutions to support the integration of internationally trained health professionals (ITHPs) into FMCs.1 This brief summarizes the study’s main findings and outlines the challenges and needs of ITHPs in francophone communities. This study is directly related to the one currently being conducted by the Standing Committee on Health. card_text: Policy brief for --Reducing pressures on Canada’s healthcare industry Lok Bhattarai, PhD A stark mismatch in demand and supply of Canadian health-services is visible and is an ‘experiential reality’ rather than an ‘uncommon experience’. There is a complex relation between the augmenting healthcare demand, Canada’s immigration policy and integration of internationally trained professionals into the workforce. To address this problem, Canada can undertake several smaller fixes which altogether can produce greater impact in the long run. Below, I have discussed some of the cost-effective fixes which I believe can yield impressive results. Regulating doctor appointments and walk-ins: It is often reported that many family doctors or the doctors offering walk-ins often ask their patients to limit on only one health issue to consult per visit. In other words, doctors are reluctant to discuss more than one issue with the patients in one visit regardless of how minor or interconnected those issues are. This is one of the practices that instead of resolving healthcare burden is contributing to escalating artificial burden in the industry. Consequently, number of visits multiplies as patients may need to visit doctors more than once (maybe several times) where the issues could have been consulted in the first visit in a matter of an additional few more minutes. A strict regulatory instruction can effectively resolve this malpractice so that doctors, except in reasonable cases, will overview all relevant issues a patient wants to discuss in one visit. card_text: Impact of Immigration Policy on Health Care in Canada, with a Focus on Nurse Practitioners Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health (HESA) Submitted by: [ORG] (NPAC-AIIPC) Dr. Laura Housden, PhD, MN-NP(F), President Dr. Stan Marchuk, DNP, MN-NP(F), Chief Executive Officer Date: December 8, 2025 1. Introduction The [ORG] (NPAC-AIIPC) submits this brief to inform the Standing Committee on Health’s study on the impact of immigration policy on Canada’s health care system, with specific attention to nurse practitioners (NPs). Canada is experiencing sustained health workforce shortages and growing unmet needs in primary care, mental health, chronic disease management, and rural and remote services. Nurse practitioners, advanced practice nurses who independently assess, diagnose, order and interpret tests, prescribe, and manage care across the life span, are a proven way to expand access and improve quality. Federal immigration policy increasingly recognizes health workers, including NPs, as a priority occupation. However, misalignment between immigration pathways, credential recognition, and the availability of funded NP positions contributes to underutilization of internationally educated health professionals (IEHPs) and missed opportunities to improve access to care. 2. Background: Nurse Practitioners in Canada 2.1 Historical development The NP role was introduced in Canada in the 1960s to respond to provider shortages and improve access in northern, rural, and remote communities. Early programs prepared experienced nurses to provide comprehensive primary care in settings where no other providers were available. card_text: Brief on Addressing Workforce Challenges in Respiratory Therapy Written Submission to the Standing Committee on Health (HESA) Submitted by the [ORG] ([ORG]) November 2025 [CONTACT] www.[ORG].com Introduction Respiratory therapists are regulated health professionals who are essential in Canada’s acute care, critical care, primary care, and community-based health systems. They support disease management in primary care for conditions such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), provide critical care in Canadian hospitals, and deliver ongoing care in the community for patients requiring complex respiratory disease management including home oxygen and mechanical ventilation. There are approximately 12,000 respiratory therapists working in Canada.1 Even before the COVID pandemic, and increasingly in its aftermath, Canada’s Occupational Projection System (COPS) Occupational Outlook identified a “Strong Risk of Shortage” of respiratory therapists between 2024-2033 at a national level. This shortage is likely to intensify: As frontline care providers to critically ill patients, the COVID pandemic has taken its toll on respiratory therapists. Alarmingly, respiratory therapists are showing worryingly high rates or signs of PTSD, symptoms of depression, and anxiety or stress. During the pandemic, more than 25% of respiratory therapists in Canada who were surveyed reported that they were considering leaving healthcare.12 Respiratory therapists are essential for addressing backlogs and access challenges across Canadian health systems. A shortage in the respiratory therapist workforce threatens Canadians’ access to essential healthcare, particularly lifesaving emergency and critical care, and chronic disease management. Ensuring people living in Canada have timely access to these experts is essential for improving access

card_text: Submission to the Standing Committee on Health for the Study on the Impact of Immigration on Health Care November 28, 2025 Section 1: Introduction A strong and sustainable healthcare workforce is essential to delivering high-quality cancer care in Canada. Healthcare providers – including those on the frontlines and in supportive roles – bring a range of skills and expertise that directly impact access, quality and continuity of care. With over 80 professions involved in cancer care, ensuring an adequate, well-trained and well-distributed workforce is a critical priority which can be aided by strategic immigration policy. Canada’s healthcare system is facing unprecedented pressures due to an aging population, rising cancer incidence and persistent gaps in workforce distribution. These challenges are especially acute in rural and remote communities, where access to timely and specialized care is often limited. As the demand for cancer services grows, it becomes increasingly important to ensure that our healthcare workforce is not only sufficient in number but also equipped with the diverse skills and professional competencies needed to serve everyone in Canada. Strategic immigration policy, alongside federal investments in training and workforce mobility, is essential to building a resilient system that can adapt to evolving healthcare needs. card_text: [ORG] submits this brief to the Standing Committee on Health to highlight the importance of sonographers in the Canadian healthcare system and the barriers to internationally trained professionals. Executive Summary: Diagnostic medicine is a vital but often less recognized component of the Canadian healthcare system, which has been increasingly under strain for the last five years. One important aspect of the diagnostic ecosystem in Canada is sonography. Sonography is the practice of using ultrasound as a medical investigation to assess organs, tissues, and blood flow inside the body. Diagnostic medical sonographers perform at least 10 million ultrasound scans in Canada each year. These highly skilled professionals are a vital part of the care economy and consistently enable improved health outcomes for Canadians, from diagnosis through patient care. Sonographers have been bearing the brunt of a medical system under strain, resulting in a shortage of Canadian-trained sonographers who are ready to enter active practice. However, there is a solution to this issue in supporting and funding programs for internationally trained medical professionals to transition into Canadian sonography practice. Current Situation: Sonography is practiced differently around the world. In most countries, sonography is performed by physicians who have the essential skills to become sonographers in Canada, should they wish to do so. With fundamental medical skills in place, most only need to familiarize themselves with Canadian practice norms and standards and acclimate to the Canadian health care system to succeed. These candidates do not require the same level of education and upskilling

card_text: Submission to the Standing Committee on Health Immigration, IEHP Integration, and Solutions to Accelerate Workforce Entry Submitted by [ORG] ([ORG]) and [ORG] November 2025 Executive Summary Canada is facing one of the most significant health human resource (HHR) shortages in its history. At the same time, nearly 260,000 internationally educated health professionals (IEHPs) currently reside in Canada—yet only 58% are working in health-related occupations. Although Canada’s immigration system successfully attracts highly skilled health professionals, our regulatory, licensure, and workforce systems do not integrate them efficiently or effectively. This represents a profound missed opportunity at a time when Canada urgently needs physicians, nurses, pharmacists, dentists, and other essential providers. Evidence from the [ORG] ([ORG]) and its partners, including the PEGASUS Institute, shows that IEHPs experience fragmented licensure pathways, outdated or inaccurate information, reduced newcomer supports due to federal program cuts, and limited access to mentoring or professional networks. These challenges delay their entry into the workforce, erode clinical skills, and create financial and emotional strain for newcomers and their families. While reforms to immigration, regulatory, and licensing systems are underway, IEHPs who are already in Canada—and those who have begun their immigration journey—require targeted support to navigate the system, progress toward licensure, and optimize their contributions to Canada’s health workforce. This submission outlines key barriers and presents three evidence-based recommendations: 1. Expand wrap-around supports such as mentoring, capacity-building, and social capital development for IEHPs already living in Canada. 2. Establish and fund a centralized, continuously updated national IEHP Resource Hub, ideally

card_text: Briefing Note to the Standing Committee on Health (HESA) Topic: Impact of Immigration Policy on Healthcare and Barriers to Integrating Internationally Trained Health Professionals Submitted by: [ORG] ([ORG]) Executive Summary Rural, remote, northern, and Indigenous communities continue to face significant physician shortages that limit access to primary care, emergency services, and ongoing management of chronic illness. Internationally trained physicians (ITPs) could help meet these needs, but federal immigration pathways and credential-recognition processes are not consistently aligned with provincial licensure or rural workforce requirements. Streamlining federal pathways for licensure-ready physicians and strengthening assessment and rural readiness supports would improve timely access to care for communities in need. The recommendations that follow focus on practical federal levers that support equitable healthcare access while respecting provincial jurisdiction. 1. Issue Rural and remote communities experience recurring gaps in physician services, including limited primary care access, temporary emergency department closures, and reduced continuity of care. The Globe and Mail reported that Canada saw 47,500 ER closure days between 2019 and June 2025, almost all of which were in rural communities and many of which were related to physician shortages. These challenges undermine patient outcomes and place additional strain on local health systems and impact community wellbeing. ITPS are essential to addressing these gaps. Many ITPs arrive with extensive experience and are ready to practice broad‑scope generalist medicine—the foundation of rural healthcare. I. Introduction The [ORG] ([ORG]) represents all 19 medical schools in the country and was pleased to provide testimony at the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health hearings on November 20, 2025. The purpose of this written submission is to build upon the important topics and questions raised at that hearing, and to provide the Committee with: - Key definitions and data points - Insights into the lack of capacity in undergraduate medical education - Ways to integrate more IMGs and FTPs into Canada’s existing training system II. Key Definitions Canadian Medical Graduates (CMGs): • Canadian citizens and permanent residents are eligible to apply for undergraduate medical seats in Canada’s 18 (soon to be 19)1 medical schools. card_text: [ORG] submission Brief on the impact of immigration policy on health care Submission to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health (HESA) November [CONTACT] Towers, suite 500, Ottawa, Ontario [CONTACT] The [ORG] ([ORG]) respectfully submits this brief to the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health for its study on the impact of immigration policy on health care and barriers to integrating internationally trained professionals. As the voice of physicians and medical learners across the country, the [ORG] has seen the human impact of a health system under growing strain – from patients waiting too long for care to colleagues struggling to stay in practice. This is a reality felt by more than 6.5 million Canadians who have no family doctor to turn to. We have also seen skilled physicians trained abroad who are eager to help, but unable because of the barriers that stand in their way. The [ORG] views this study as a natural extension of the conversation our country must have about health workforce planning. We testified before the health committee in 2022 about solutions to the health workforce crisis. Three years later, health workforce planning remains fragmented while demand for care continues to climb. More than 13,000 internationally trained physicians in Canada are not working in their field. We must do more to recognize medical talent already here – and to attract, welcome and retain more from around the world. card_text: Written Submission to the Standing Committee on Health Addressing Workforce Challenges in the Oral Health Workforce Submitted by the [ORG] October 2025 Addressing Workforce Challenges in the Oral Health Sector Across Canada, the oral healthcare sector is grappling with serious human resources challenges, similar to those seen across the broader healthcare system. Dental clinics are struggling to hire and retain dental assistants and dental hygienists. Even prior to the COVID-19 pandemic, dentists were reporting significant concerns regarding their ability to recruit and retain well-qualified staff, particularly dental assistants. By 2019, roughly one-third of dental offices had vacant dental assistant positions, and this issue was exacerbated by the pandemic. Further, as Canada’s population continues to age, there will be an increase in demand and added pressures for dental offices. According to a recent Statistics Canada survey, 80% of dental offices in Canada are reporting staffing challenges. 46.5% of dental offices are having difficulties in recruiting dental assistants and 50.% are having difficulties in recruiting dental hygienists. In 2023 alone, over 7,200 job vacancies (dental assistants and dental hygienists) went unfilled across Canada. The Canadian Dental Care Plan (CDCP), which fully launched for all eligible populations earlier this year, will add to these staffing pressures on dental offices. [ORG] estimates that there will be a need for 2,300 additional dental assistants and 1,500 additional dental hygienists due to the CDCP alone.
